name: ade20k
summary:
Build a semantic segmentation model for scene parsing on ADE20K. Given an RGB image
of an indoor or outdoor scene, predict a per-pixel semantic label from 150 categories
(wall, floor, sky, person, car, chair, tree, etc.). The 150 classes mix "stuff"
(wall, sky, floor, road) and "things" (person, car, chair) and are highly imbalanced
— a handful of classes dominate the pixel count while long-tail classes appear in
only a small fraction of training images.
description:
Build a semantic segmentation model for scene parsing on ADE20K. Given an RGB image
of an indoor or outdoor scene, predict a per-pixel semantic label from 150 categories
(wall, floor, sky, person, car, chair, tree, etc.). The 150 classes mix "stuff"
(wall, sky, floor, road) and "things" (person, car, chair) and are highly imbalanced
— a handful of classes dominate the pixel count while long-tail classes appear in
only a small fraction of training images.

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - meta.json: JSON metadata with fields
      "num_classes": 150
      "ignore_index": 255                    (label value to ignore in loss and mIoU)
      "class_names": list of 150 strings     ("wall", "building", "sky", "floor", ...,
                                              "flag"); class_names[i] is the name of
                                              the 0-indexed class i
      "mean":       [0.485, 0.456, 0.406]    (RGB channel means)
      "std":        [0.229, 0.224, 0.225]    (RGB channel stds)
      "crop_size":  512
  - images/training/: 20,210 RGB JPEG files, 8-bit per channel.
      Filenames: ADE_train_00000001.jpg ... ADE_train_00020210.jpg (zero-padded 8 digits).
      Resolution varies per image: shorter side typically 300-800 px, longer side up to
      ~2500 px; aspect ratios span roughly 0.5-2.0. Images are NOT pre-resized, so any
      dataloader must handle variable H×W.
  - annotations/training/: 20,210 single-channel uint8 PNG masks.
      Filenames match the corresponding image (ADE_train_*.png); each mask's H×W
      matches its image's H×W exactly. Pixel value semantics in the raw files:
          0          = unlabeled pixel (exclude from loss and mIoU)
          1 ... 150  = class indices (1=wall, 2=building, 3=sky, ..., 150=flag)
      The raw labels are 1-indexed. To align with a 0-indexed 150-way classifier,
      subtract 1 from every label and remap the original 0 pixels to ignore_index (255).
      Equivalently: raw pixel value k (for k in 1..150) corresponds to class_names[k-1].
  - images/validation/:      2,000 RGB JPEG files (same resolution distribution as train).
  - annotations/validation/: 2,000 uint8 PNG masks (same encoding as train).
      This is the official MIT Scene Parsing Benchmark val split and is the
      community-standard evaluation set (Papers With Code, MMSegmentation leaderboard).
      Labels here are visible to the agent — you may use them for model selection,
      early stopping, or hyperparameter tuning. The evaluator scores on the same split.

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>

Input: --data_path is $FARBENCH_TEST_DATA_DIR. It contains meta.json and
       images/validation/*.jpg — 2,000 JPEG images at their ORIGINAL resolution.
       Ground-truth masks are stored separately under labels/ for the evaluator.
       Predict one semantic mask per image, ALSO at the image's original H×W
       (do NOT output a fixed-size mask; the evaluator compares pixel-for-pixel).
Output: Two artifacts:
  1. A directory of PNG mask files saved NEXT TO the output JSON file.
     Directory name = output filename (without ".json") + "_masks/".
     Example: if --output is /results/eval_predictions.json, save masks to
     /results/eval_predictions_masks/ADE_val_00000001.png, ADE_val_00000002.png, ...
     (one .png per input image, filename stem matching the image's stem).
     Each PNG is a single-channel uint8 image whose pixel values are class indices
     0..149 — i.e., already 0-indexed; do NOT shift back to 1..150.
  2. The JSON file at --output with content:
       {"predictions_dir": "<absolute_path_to_mask_directory>/"}
     pointing to that mask directory.
  This two-step format avoids storing huge pixel arrays inside JSON.

name: aime_math_rl
summary:
Use reinforcement learning to fine-tune an LLM for AIME-style math reasoning.
Maximize exact-match accuracy on AIME 2024 + 2025 (60 competition problems).
description:
Use reinforcement learning to fine-tune an LLM for AIME-style math reasoning.
Maximize exact-match accuracy on AIME 2024 + 2025 (60 competition problems).

Training source is `Skywork/Skywork-OR1-RL-Data` (math split, ~105K
verifiable problems, de-contaminated against AIME 2024/2025).

## Data Format

Training data at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - train.jsonl           (~100K rows from Skywork-OR1 math)
  - val.jsonl             (~5K deterministic holdout)
  - sample_train.jsonl    (256-row debug subset)
  - dataset_info.json

The Qwen3-4B base model is NOT in /data. It is pre-cached in the image's
Hugging Face cache and loaded by the canonical id `Qwen/Qwen3-4B` (no
network required, see "Base model" section below).

Train row schema: {id, problem, expected_answer, data_source, messages,
difficulty, raw_ground_truth}. `difficulty` is the Skywork 7B-proxy score
in [0, 16] (higher = harder; chosen because Qwen3-4B is the base model).

Test data at $FARBENCH_TEST_DATA_DIR:
  - test.jsonl          (60 rows: AIME24 + AIME25, fields: id, problem, year)
  - test_answers.json   (hidden, evaluator only)

All AIME answers are integers in [0, 999].

## Predict Script Contract

Create `predict.py`:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <ckpt_dir> --output <output.json>

Output JSON: {"predictions": [{"id": "aime24-...", "answer": "123"}, ...]}
The evaluator tolerates `\boxed{...}`, surrounding text, and zero-padding.

name: assist2009_kt
summary:
Knowledge Tracing on ASSISTments 2009: predict whether a student will
correctly answer their next question, given their interaction history.
description:
Knowledge Tracing on ASSISTments 2009: predict whether a student will
correctly answer their next question, given their interaction history.

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - train.pt: dict with keys:
      "skill_ids": int64 tensor [N, max_seq_len] — skill index per step (0-based)
      "corrects":  int64 tensor [N, max_seq_len] — 1 if correct, 0 if wrong
      "lengths":   int64 tensor [N]              — actual sequence length per student
      "num_skills": int                           — total number of unique skills
  - val.pt: same format

Each row is one student's interaction sequence, zero-padded to max_seq_len.
At each step t, the student attempts skill skill_ids[t] and gets result corrects[t].
The task: given steps 0..t-1, predict corrects[t].

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <ckpt> --output <output.json>

Input: test_data_dir contains test.pt with same format as train.pt.
Output: JSON file with format {"predictions": [0.82, 0.15, 0.67, ...]}
        A flat list of predicted probabilities (float, 0-1) for each VALID
        prediction step across ALL sequences.

Valid prediction steps: for a sequence of length L, there are L-1 predictions
(predicting step 1 from step 0, step 2 from steps 0-1, etc.).
Targets are corrects[:, 1:] (i.e. shifted by one step).
Let T = corrects.shape[1] (the padded sequence length in the .pt file).
The mask for valid prediction positions is:
    mask = torch.arange(T - 1).unsqueeze(0) < (lengths.unsqueeze(1) - 1)
Total number of predictions = mask.sum().item().
Output predictions in sample order (sequence 0 first, then sequence 1, etc.).

name: asvspoof2021_la
summary: Build an audio deepfake detector for the ASVspoof 2021 Logical Access task — a binary classifier that distinguishes bonafide (genuine) speech from spoofed (TTS/voice-conversion) speech. The key challenge is generalization: models are trained on clean audio but evaluated on speech transmitted through various telephony codecs (a-law, G.722, GSM, OPUS, etc.).
description:
Build an audio deepfake detector for the ASVspoof 2021 Logical Access task — a binary classifier that distinguishes bonafide (genuine) speech from spoofed (TTS/voice-conversion) speech. The key challenge is generalization: models are trained on clean audio but evaluated on speech transmitted through various telephony codecs (a-law, G.722, GSM, OPUS, etc.).

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - train/flac/:       25,380 training .flac files from ASVspoof 2019 LA (16 kHz mono, 16-bit, ~1-15s, typical 3-5s)
  - dev/flac/:         24,844 development .flac files (same audio specs)
  - train_protocol.txt: 25,380 lines, 5 space-separated columns:
      col 1 — speaker_id      (e.g. "LA_0079"; 20 unique train speakers)
      col 2 — utterance_id    (e.g. "LA_T_1138215"; corresponds to file train/flac/<utterance_id>.flac)
      col 3 — environment_id  (always "-" in LA task, can be ignored)
      col 4 — attack_type     ("-" for bonafide, "A01"-"A06" for the 6 known TTS/VC attacks)
      col 5 — label           ("bonafide" or "spoof")
  - dev_protocol.txt:  24,844 lines, same 5-column format. Dev speakers (20) are disjoint from train.

Class distribution (heavily imbalanced):
  train: 2,580 bonafide / 22,800 spoof  (~10% / 90%)
  dev:   2,548 bonafide / 22,296 spoof  (~10% / 90%)

Example train_protocol.txt rows:
  LA_0079 LA_T_1138215 - - bonafide
  LA_0079 LA_T_1004644 - A01 spoof

Test set: ~18,000 utterances subsampled from the ASVspoof 2021 LA eval set (codec-degraded
audio: a-law / mu-law / G.722 / GSM / OPUS / clean conditions), stratified by (label, attack_id)
to preserve both the ~10% bonafide ratio and the 13-attack distribution. Test attacks (A07-A19)
do NOT overlap with train/dev attacks (A01-A06), and test speakers are disjoint from train/dev —
this is an out-of-domain generalization benchmark across 3 axes: unseen attacks, unseen channels,
unseen speakers.

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>

Input: test_data_dir contains:
  - test.txt:  one utterance_id per line (e.g. "LA_E_1000854"); defines the order of predictions.
  - flac/:     18,000 .flac files named <utterance_id>.flac (16 kHz mono, same format as train).
  No labels are visible to the agent at test time.

Output: JSON file with format {"predictions": [1.037, -0.234, 0.567, ...]}
        One float score per test utterance, in the same order as test.txt.
        Higher score = more likely bonafide. Scores are unbounded (logits or any monotonic
        score work — EER is threshold-free).

name: bigcodebench_codegen
summary:
Fine-tune a code-generation model on Nvidia OpenCodeInstruct and evaluate on
BigCodeBench-Full (Complete variant) with pass@1.
description:
Fine-tune a code-generation model on Nvidia OpenCodeInstruct and evaluate on
BigCodeBench-Full (Complete variant) with pass@1.

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - train.jsonl: ~195,000 training samples, JSONL format. Each line:
      {
        "id": "...",
        "prompt": "coding problem description (from OpenCodeInstruct `input`)",
        "completion": "python solution; often wrapped in a ```python ... ``` markdown fence",
        "source": "nvidia/OpenCodeInstruct",
        "domain": "generic|algorithmic|...",
        "average_test_score": 0.0-1.0,        // fraction of unit tests passing (sanity signal)
        "tests_execution_status": "pass|fail|error|unknown"
      }
  - val.jsonl: ~5,000 validation samples, same schema as train.jsonl
  - dataset_info.json: metadata about the dataset preparation

NOTE on training data quality:
  Samples are NOT pre-filtered. You may optionally filter on
  `average_test_score` (e.g. >= 0.9) or `tests_execution_status == "pass"`
  to keep only high-quality examples.

NOTE on completion format:
  Completions in train/val retain the upstream markdown code fences
  (```python\\n...\\n```). Two implications:
    1. During training, the model will learn to emit fenced code.
    2. Your predict.py MUST strip the fence before writing to
       predictions.json, otherwise the evaluator feeds ``` into Python
       and every test case will fail with SyntaxError.

Test data is at $FARBENCH_TEST_DATA_DIR:
  - test_prompts.jsonl: 1,140 BigCodeBench-Full problems, each line:
      {"id": "BigCodeBench/0", "prompt": "...", "starter_code": "...", "entry_point": "task_func"}
  - test_cases.jsonl: ground-truth unittest test cases (used by evaluator only)

Each test problem uses an average of 2.8 libraries from 139 available libraries
(77 stdlib + 62 third-party). The prompt includes a verbose docstring (~33 lines avg)
with interactive examples. The model must generate a complete function body that
passes all test cases (~5.6 tests per problem, 99% branch coverage).

The evaluator runs each problem in a subprocess with a 240s per-problem
timeout (matches upstream BigCodeBench), with 16-way thread-pool parallelism
and a 25-minute global exec wall-clock deadline. Cases that hit either limit
count as failures.  Typical exec wall-clock: ~5-15 minutes on a 32+ core
host. Override via env vars BIGCODEBENCH_EVAL_{TIMEOUT_SECONDS,WORKERS,
DEADLINE_SECONDS}.

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <ckpt_dir> --output <output.json>

Input: test_data_dir contains test_prompts.jsonl with fields: id, prompt, starter_code, entry_point
Output: JSON file with format {"predictions": [{"id": "BigCodeBench/0", "completion": "def task_func(...):\n    ..."}]}
        One completion per problem, in any order. Each completion must be the raw
        function body / module-level code — NO markdown fences.

name: cifar100lt
summary: Build an image classifier for CIFAR-100-LT, a long-tailed version of CIFAR-100 with imbalance ratio 100. The training set follows an exponential class distribution (500 → 5 samples per class across 100 classes). The test set is balanced (100 images per class). The challenge is to achieve high accuracy despite extreme class imbalance.
description:
Build an image classifier for CIFAR-100-LT, a long-tailed version of CIFAR-100 with imbalance ratio 100. The training set follows an exponential class distribution (500 → 5 samples per class across 100 classes). The test set is balanced (100 images per class). The challenge is to achieve high accuracy despite extreme class imbalance.

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - train.pt: dict with keys "images" (uint8 tensor, shape [N, 3, 32, 32]) and "labels" (int64 tensor, shape [N])
  - val.pt:   same format as train.pt
  - class_counts.json: dict mapping class index (str) to training sample count, useful for designing class-balanced losses

Images are raw pixel values 0-255 (uint8), in CHW format. N is ~10,626 for train (long-tailed) and ~1,000 for val (balanced subset). Labels are integers 0-99.

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>

Input: test_data_dir contains test.pt with same format as train.pt (images + labels).
       Your script should ONLY use the "images" key (labels are ground truth).
Output: JSON file with format {"predictions": [42, 7, 99, 0, ...]}
        One predicted class index (int 0-99) per test sample, in order.

name: cifar100n
summary: Build an image classifier for CIFAR-100N, a variant of CIFAR-100 where training labels contain ~40% real-world human annotation noise (collected via Amazon Mechanical Turk). The test set has clean (correct) labels. The challenge is to achieve high accuracy despite severe label noise.
description:
Build an image classifier for CIFAR-100N, a variant of CIFAR-100 where training labels contain ~40% real-world human annotation noise (collected via Amazon Mechanical Turk). The test set has clean (correct) labels. The challenge is to achieve high accuracy despite severe label noise.

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - train.pt: dict with keys "images" (uint8 tensor, shape [N, 3, 32, 32]) and "labels" (int64 tensor, shape [N]) — labels contain ~40% noise
  - val.pt:   same format as train.pt — labels also contain ~40% noise

N is ~45,000 for train and ~5,000 for val. Images are raw pixel values 0-255 (uint8), in CHW format. Labels are integers 0-99, but approximately 40% are incorrect human annotations. The noise is instance-dependent (harder images are more likely to be mislabeled).

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>

Input: test_data_dir contains test.pt with same format as train.pt (images + labels).
       Your script should ONLY use the "images" key (labels are ground truth).
Output: JSON file with format {"predictions": [42, 7, 99, 0, ...]}
        One predicted class index (int 0-99) per test sample, in order.

name: climsim_lowres
summary: Build a regression model to emulate subgrid-scale atmospheric physics (clouds, convection, radiation, turbulence) from the ClimSim dataset. Given a single atmospheric column state, predict the physical tendencies and fluxes that a high-resolution climate simulation would produce. This is an offline (single-timestep) emulation task on the subsampled low-resolution (11.5°, 384-column) version of the E3SM-MMF climate model output.
description:
Build a regression model to emulate subgrid-scale atmospheric physics (clouds, convection, radiation, turbulence) from the ClimSim dataset. Given a single atmospheric column state, predict the physical tendencies and fluxes that a high-resolution climate simulation would produce. This is an offline (single-timestep) emulation task on the subsampled low-resolution (11.5°, 384-column) version of the E3SM-MMF climate model output.

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - train_input.npy: float32 [~10,100,000, 124] — subsampled training inputs (every 7th timestep from years 1-7, all 384 grid columns). Pre-normalized.
  - train_target.npy: float32 [~10,100,000, 128] — corresponding training targets. Pre-normalized.
  - val_input.npy: float32 [~1,440,000, 124] — validation inputs (year 8, stride=7). Pre-normalized.
  - val_target.npy: float32 [~1,440,000, 128] — validation targets. Pre-normalized.

Input features (124 total, pre-normalized):
  - state_t: temperature profile (60 vertical levels)
  - state_q0001: specific humidity profile (60 vertical levels)
  - state_ps: surface pressure (1 scalar)
  - pbuf_SOLIN: solar insolation (1 scalar)
  - pbuf_LHFLX: surface latent heat flux (1 scalar)
  - pbuf_SHFLX: surface sensible heat flux (1 scalar)

Output targets (128 total, pre-normalized):
  - ptend_t: temperature tendency (60 vertical levels)
  - ptend_q0001: specific humidity tendency (60 vertical levels)
  - cam_out_NETSW: net shortwave radiation (1 scalar)
  - cam_out_FLWDS: downward longwave radiation (1 scalar)
  - cam_out_PRECSC: stratiform precipitation (1 scalar)
  - cam_out_PRECC: convective precipitation (1 scalar)
  - cam_out_SOLS, cam_out_SOLL, cam_out_SOLSD, cam_out_SOLLD: solar radiation components (4 scalars)

All data is pre-normalized (zero mean, unit variance). Train and predict in normalized space. The evaluation computes R² in normalized space, matching the protocol from the NeurIPS 2023 paper (Yu et al., 2023).

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>

Input: test_data_dir contains scoring_input.npy — float32 [N_test, 124], pre-normalized test inputs.
Output: Two things:
  1. Save predictions as a numpy file at <output_stem>_predictions.npy (where <output_stem> is the --output path without .json extension), shape [N_test, 128], float32, in normalized space.
  2. Write the JSON file at --output path: {"predictions_file": "<absolute_path_to_npy>"}

name: cogniplan
summary:
Train an exploration planner for autonomous robot mapping in unknown 2D
environments. The robot must efficiently navigate and map unknown spaces
(rooms, tunnels, outdoor areas) while maximising exploration coverage and
minimising travel distance.
description:
Train an exploration planner for autonomous robot mapping in unknown 2D
environments. The robot must efficiently navigate and map unknown spaces
(rooms, tunnels, outdoor areas) while maximising exploration coverage and
minimising travel distance.

Primary metric: exploration_score (higher is better) — combines success rate and
path efficiency:
  exploration_score = (1/N) * Σ [ S_i * (300 / d_i) ]
where S_i is binary success (explored_rate > 0.9999), d_i is travel distance (m),
and 300 m is a reference distance. Failed episodes contribute 0. Successful
episodes with shorter paths score higher.
Secondary metrics: success_rate, travel_distance.

## Task Description

A robot starts at a fixed position (marked in the map) in an unknown 2D occupancy
grid map. At each planning step the robot:
  1. Scans surroundings via a 360° raycasting sensor (16 m range, 0.5° resolution).
  2. Updates the explored belief map (FREE=255, OCCUPIED=1, UNKNOWN=127).
  3. Selects a neighbouring node from the exploration graph to move to.
  4. Moves to that node; travel distance is accumulated in metres.

Navigation uses a sparse graph of free nodes spaced NODE_RESOLUTION=3.6 m apart.
Each node has a utility value (number of observable frontier cells within 12.8 m).
The episode ends when explored_rate > 0.9999, or no nodes have utility > 0,
or 128 planning steps are exhausted.

## Environment

The CogniPlan exploration environment is pre-installed. It provides a
2D grid-based simulation with no rendering requirements.

Constants (matching official CogniPlan):
  CELL_SIZE        = 0.4 m/cell
  NODE_RESOLUTION  = 3.6 m
  SENSOR_RANGE     = 16.0 m
  SUCCESS_THRESHOLD = 0.9999
  MAX_EPISODE_STEP = 128

Quick-start example:

  from cogniplan_env import ExplorationEnv

  env = ExplorationEnv(map_path="path/to/map.png")
  obs = env.reset()
  # obs keys:
  #   "global_map":    uint8 (H, W) — belief map (FREE=255, OCCUPIED=1, UNKNOWN=127)
  #   "robot_pos":     float32 (2,) — robot (x, y) in metres
  #   "frontiers":     list[(float, float)] — frontier coords in metres
  #   "nodes":         list[dict] — graph nodes {coords, utility, visited, neighbors}
  #   "neighbors":     list[(float, float)] — reachable neighbour node coords (metres)
  #   "explored_rate": float — fraction of free cells explored so far

  for step in range(128):
      target = select_waypoint(obs)   # choose from obs["neighbors"]
      obs, info = env.step(target)    # target is (x, y) in metres
      if info["done"]:
          break

  metrics = env.get_metrics()
  # {"explored_rate": 0.9999, "travel_distance": 245.3, "n_steps": 87, "success": True}

## Data Format

Training maps are at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - maps_train/     — training maps (PNG grayscale images)
      room/         — indoor room-like environments
      tunnel/       — narrow corridor environments
      outdoor/      — open outdoor-like environments
  - meta.json       — metadata:
      "n_maps_train": 6000, "n_maps_eval": 150,
      "env_types": ["room", "tunnel", "outdoor"],
      "sensor_range": 16.0, "max_steps": 128,
      "success_threshold": 0.9999

Each map is a grayscale PNG: pixel 255=free, pixel 127=wall, pixel 208=start position.
The environment binarises internally: free (>150 or 50–80) → FREE=255, else OCCUPIED=1.

## Predict Script Contract

You MUST create predict.py in /workspace that:
  1. Loads your trained model from --checkpoint directory.
  2. Runs evaluation on all maps listed in eval_config.json.
  3. Writes a JSON results file to --output.

Invocation:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <ckpt_dir> --output <output.json>

Input: --data_path contains:
  eval_config.json  — evaluation specification
  maps_eval/        — evaluation maps (room/, tunnel/, outdoor/)

Output JSON format (required):
  {
    "explored_rate": 0.95,
    "travel_distance": 280.5,
    "success_rate": 0.72,
    "per_episode": [
      {"map_path": "room/map_001.png", "env_type": "room",
       "success": 1, "travel_distance": 245.3, "explored_rate": 0.9999},
      ...
    ],
    "per_type": {
      "room":    {"explored_rate": 0.97, "travel_distance": 220.1, "success_rate": 0.84},
      "tunnel":  {"explored_rate": 0.94, "travel_distance": 310.2, "success_rate": 0.68},
      "outdoor": {"explored_rate": 0.93, "travel_distance": 311.3, "success_rate": 0.64}
    },
    "total_episodes": 150
  }

per_episode:      REQUIRED — list of per-episode results. Each entry must include
                  "success" (0 or 1), "travel_distance" (metres), and "explored_rate".
                  The evaluator computes exploration_score from this data.
explored_rate:    mean fraction of free space explored across all episodes.
travel_distance:  mean distance traveled in metres across *successful* episodes only
                  (explored_rate > 0.9999). If no episodes succeed, report 0.0.
success_rate:     fraction of episodes where explored_rate > 0.9999 within 128 steps.

## Execution Priority

Submit evaluation after each training run to track progress.
Iterate based on eval results — do not train multiple times without evaluating.

## Constraints

- Network architecture is free but total parameters must be <= 50M.
- You may use any planning/RL algorithm.
- The environment is deterministic given a map and start position.

name: crohme_hmer
summary:
Handwritten Mathematical Expression Recognition on CROHME 2019: given a
grayscale image of a handwritten mathematical expression, output the
corresponding LaTeX token sequence.
description:
Handwritten Mathematical Expression Recognition on CROHME 2019: given a
grayscale image of a handwritten mathematical expression, output the
corresponding LaTeX token sequence.

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - meta.json: metadata with fields:
      "n_train": 9993
      "n_val": 986      (CROHME 2014 test — the literature-standard val set)
      "n_test": 1199
      "vocab_size": number of unique LaTeX tokens (~134)
      "max_img_h": max image height across all rendered expressions
      "max_img_w": max image width across all rendered expressions
  - vocab.txt: one LaTeX token per line; line i (0-based) is token id i.
      Reserved tokens: <pad>=0, <sos>=1, <eos>=2, <unk>=3.
  - train/images/<id>.png: grayscale (mode='L') PNG of the handwritten expression,
      black strokes on white background, variable size (typically 40-400 px tall,
      60-1200 px wide).
  - train/labels.txt: one line per training sample, tab-separated:
      "<id>\t<space-separated LaTeX tokens>"
      Example: "train_0001\t\\frac { 1 } { 2 } = 0 . 5"
  - val/images/<id>.png and val/labels.txt: same format. val = CROHME 2014
      test set (the literature-standard validation set used by CoMER, CAN,
      BTTR, PosFormer). It is shipped inside the official training archive.

The LaTeX tokens are already pre-tokenized (whitespace-separated symbols)
and canonicalized with the CoMER/CAN community's convention:
  - \mathrm{sin|cos|tan|log|lim|max|det|...} has been rewritten to \sin|\cos|...
  - Redundant single-leaf-token braces have been stripped: {C} → C, {\sum} → \sum,
    but multi-token braces like {2n} stay braced. So for example
    `C={\\sum }_{n=1}{c}_{n}{n}^{2}` becomes
    `C = \\sum _ { n = 1 } c _ n n ^ 2`.
A token is either a single character (digits, letters, =, +, -, etc.) or a
LaTeX command (\frac, \sum, \sqrt, \alpha, etc.) or a grouping brace ({ or }).

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <ckpt> --output <output.json>

Input: --data_path is a DIRECTORY containing:
         images/<id>.png — 1199 grayscale test images (no labels visible)
         meta.json, vocab.txt — same as training (for consistent vocabulary)
Output: JSON file with format:
  {"predictions": [
      {"id": "test_0001", "latex": "\\frac { 1 } { 2 }"},
      {"id": "test_0002", "latex": "a + b = c"},
      ...
  ]}
The predictions list length must equal the number of test images. Each
entry's "latex" is a LaTeX string. The evaluator applies the same
canonicalization as on ground-truth labels (see description above), so
equivalent styles like `\\frac{1}{2}`, `\\frac { 1 } { 2 }`, and `\\frac 1 2`
all compare equal. The only requirement is that LaTeX commands and the
following argument be lexically separated (e.g., write `\\sin x`, not
`\\sinx`, because the latter parses as one undefined command). Order within
the list does not matter — entries are matched by "id".

name: domainnet_quickdraw
summary: Train a 345-way image classifier on 5 source visual domains (Clipart, Infograph, Painting, Real, Sketch) that generalizes to a held-out 6th target domain, Quickdraw — hand-drawn sketches from Google Quick, Draw!. This is the standard DomainBed leave-one-domain-out DG protocol on **DomainNet-345 cleaned version** (Peng et al., ICCV 2019). The source domains contain photographs / clipart / paintings with rich color and texture; Quickdraw contains sparse black-stroke line drawings at 300×300 RGB PNG on white background rendered from simplified vector strokes via B-spline. The domain gap is extreme — published top-1 accuracy on Quickdraw caps around 17% (ERM++ WACV'25 17.3%, DPSPG 2025 17.22%) even though the same backbones reach 60%+ on Real/Clipart targets, so very-low absolute numbers are expected and correct.
description:
Train a 345-way image classifier on 5 source visual domains (Clipart, Infograph, Painting, Real, Sketch) that generalizes to a held-out 6th target domain, Quickdraw — hand-drawn sketches from Google Quick, Draw!. This is the standard DomainBed leave-one-domain-out DG protocol on **DomainNet-345 cleaned version** (Peng et al., ICCV 2019). The source domains contain photographs / clipart / paintings with rich color and texture; Quickdraw contains sparse black-stroke line drawings at 300×300 RGB PNG on white background rendered from simplified vector strokes via B-spline. The domain gap is extreme — published top-1 accuracy on Quickdraw caps around 17% (ERM++ WACV'25 17.3%, DPSPG 2025 17.22%) even though the same backbones reach 60%+ on Real/Clipart targets, so very-low absolute numbers are expected and correct.

## Data Format

### Directory layout

Training data is at `$FARBENCH_DATA_DIR` (mounted as `/data` in the agent container):

```
$FARBENCH_DATA_DIR/
    train.txt            330,583 lines, format "<relative_path> <label>" (space-separated)
    val.txt               83,492 lines, same format; stratified 80/20 hold-out
                          of the combined source train+test splits (seed=42,
                          stratified by (domain, class))
    domain_labels.txt    330,583 lines, one integer per line; the line at index i
                          is the domain ID of train.txt line i
                          (0=clipart, 1=infograph, 2=painting, 3=real, 4=sketch)
    class_names.txt      345 lines; line index i == label index i
                          (line 0 = aircraft_carrier, line 1 = airplane,
                           line 2 = alarm_clock, line 3 = ambulance,
                           line 4 = angel, line 5 = animal_migration, line 6 = ant,
                           …, line 343 = zebra, line 344 = zigzag)
    images/
        clipart/<class_name>/<file>.jpg     48,129 files referenced (~1.3 GB)
        infograph/<class_name>/<file>.jpg   51,605 files referenced (~4.0 GB)
        painting/<class_name>/<file>.jpg    72,266 files referenced (~3.7 GB)
        real/<class_name>/<file>.jpg       172,947 files referenced (~5.6 GB)
        sketch/<class_name>/<file>.jpg      69,128 files referenced (~2.5 GB)
```

Source-domain totals: **414,075 images** (cleaned version, combined official train+test),
disk footprint ≈ **17 GB**. Split counts come from the cleaned `<domain>_{train,test}.txt`
files at `csr.bu.edu/ftp/visda/2019/multi-source/domainnet/txt/`. Note: the images/
directories may contain a few hundred additional files per domain (the raw uncleaned
zip contents); only files referenced in train.txt / val.txt are part of the protocol.

Test data is at `$FARBENCH_TEST_DATA_DIR` (also mounted as `/data` during evaluation):

```
$FARBENCH_TEST_DATA_DIR/
    test.txt             172,500 lines, format "<relative_path>" (no label, no header)
    images/
        quickdraw/<class_name>/<key_id>.png   172,500 PNG files (~439 MB total)
```

Quickdraw is **exactly 500 images per class × 345 classes = 172,500** (uniformly class-balanced — this is why the evaluator can report plain `accuracy`; balanced_accuracy differs by <1 pp in practice).

### Example lines

`train.txt` — (path, label) pairs space-separated:
```
real/airplane/real_012_000001.jpg 1
clipart/aircraft_carrier/clipart_001_000018.jpg 0
sketch/alarm_clock/sketch_003_000034.jpg 2
painting/tiger/painting_293_001234.jpg 309
infograph/wine_glass/infograph_337_000567.jpg 335
```

`test.txt` — path only, one per line:
```
quickdraw/airplane/6240401310613504.png
quickdraw/ant/5013280843857920.png
quickdraw/zebra/4986254852390912.png
```

`class_names.txt` — alphabetically ordered, underscores join multi-word names:
```
aircraft_carrier      # label 0
airplane              # label 1
alarm_clock           # label 2
ambulance             # label 3
angel                 # label 4
animal_migration      # label 5
ant                   # label 6
…
zebra                 # label 343
zigzag                # label 344
```

Class-name quirks: three classes use CamelCase with underscores (`The_Eiffel_Tower`,
`The_Great_Wall_of_China`, `The_Mona_Lisa`) and sort under capital "T" in default
Python `sorted()` order; one class is hyphenated (`t-shirt`). **Always read the
authoritative mapping from `class_names.txt`**, don't reconstruct it from scratch.

### Image format per domain

Source domains (training):
  - **Clipart** — stylized illustrations, `.jpg`, variable resolution (often ≥500 px), RGB.
  - **Infograph** — infographic-style images with text/chart overlays, `.jpg`, often 1000+ px, RGB.
  - **Painting** — fine-art paintings, `.jpg`, variable, RGB.
  - **Real** — natural photographs, `.jpg`, highest volume and resolution, RGB.
  - **Sketch** — pencil/ink sketches, `.jpg`, variable, stored as RGB JPEG (content is mostly
    near-grayscale; content color ≈ grayscale but file is 3-channel).

Target domain (evaluation):
  - **Quickdraw** — black strokes on white background rendered from Google Quick, Draw!
    simplified vector strokes via B-spline, written as `.png` at **300 × 300 RGB**
    (mode='RGB', 3 channels), avg ≈ 2.5 KB/image. Pretrained ImageNet backbones can
    read these directly; a defensive `Image.open(p).convert('RGB')` is still recommended
    in case any file downloads as grayscale.

### Image size and normalization conventions

DG papers on DomainNet uniformly use **224 × 224 RGB with ImageNet normalization**:
`mean = [0.485, 0.456, 0.406]`, `std = [0.229, 0.224, 0.225]`. Input tensor shape is
`(3, 224, 224)`, `num_classes = 345`. Any source-image resolution / aspect ratio is fine
on the encoder side; downstream shape must be 224 × 224 after your preprocessing.

### Using domain labels (optional)

`domain_labels.txt` gives per-sample domain IDs for every training example. Domain-aware
methods read it to group minibatches by domain or compute inter-domain statistics; plain
methods can ignore it.

### Practical data-loading notes

- Image extensions are **mixed**: source domains are `.jpg`, Quickdraw test is `.png`. Any
  custom file globber must include both.
- `torchvision.datasets.ImageFolder(root=images/)` on the **train** side will auto-assign
  labels by alphabetical folder order, which matches `class_names.txt` exactly — but will
  treat each domain subfolder as a top-level class, producing 5 × 345 = 1725 "classes".
  Prefer to read `train.txt` / `val.txt` directly (label is column 2) instead of using
  `ImageFolder` naively.
- Memory footprint of decoded batch: `bs × 3 × 224 × 224 × 4 bytes` → a batch of 160 is
  ~96 MB in fp32 (~48 MB in fp16). Both GPUs fit comfortably.
- **Test-set paths contain class names** (e.g., `quickdraw/airplane/…png`). Do NOT parse
  the class segment to construct predictions — the directory layout is a mirror of the
  training structure, and an honest evaluator run requires predicting from pixels alone.

## Predict Script Contract

You must create a `predict.py` in your workspace that is invoked as:

```
python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>
```

- `--data_path` points to `$FARBENCH_TEST_DATA_DIR` (i.e. `/data` inside the eval container),
  containing `test.txt` and `images/quickdraw/<class>/<id>.png`.
- `--checkpoint` is whatever artifact you saved during training (any format you choose —
  `.pt`, `.safetensors`, a directory — as long as your `predict.py` loads it).
- `--output` is the path your script must write the predictions JSON to.

**Input**: `test.txt` lists 172,500 relative image paths, one per line, no header, no labels.
Load each image, apply your eval preprocessing (224×224 resize + ImageNet normalization
is the safe default), batch through your trained model, and record the argmax class index
in `[0, 344]`.

**Output**: Write a JSON file at `--output` with exactly one top-level key `predictions`:

```json
{"predictions": [42, 0, 344, 7, 3, ...]}
```

The list length must equal the number of lines in `test.txt` (172,500), **in the same
order as test.txt**. Each entry is an `int` class index in `[0, 344]`. A length mismatch
raises `ValueError` during evaluation. The evaluator computes top-1 accuracy (primary)
and balanced_accuracy (secondary).

name: etth1_forecasting
summary: Build a multivariate time series forecasting model on ETTh1 (Electricity Transformer Temperature, hourly) to predict all 7 features 720 steps ahead from a 96-step lookback window.
description:
Build a multivariate time series forecasting model on ETTh1 (Electricity Transformer Temperature, hourly) to predict all 7 features 720 steps ahead from a 96-step lookback window.

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - train.csv: CSV with 7 columns (HUFL, HULL, MUFL, MULL, LUFL, LULL, OT), 8640 rows (12 months hourly)
  - val.csv:   same format, 2880 rows (4 months hourly)

Each row is one hourly observation. The 7 feature columns in order are: HUFL, HULL, MUFL, MULL, LUFL, LULL, OT. The first 6 are power-load features (High/Medium/Low-UseFul/UseLess) and OT is oil temperature. All values are floats in their original scale.

Standard split borders: [0, 8640, 11520, 14400] (12/4/4 months). The test region spans rows [11424, 14400) (2976 rows, including 96-step lookback before the test border). Test windows are created with seq_len=96, pred_len=720, stride=1, yielding 2161 windows.

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>

Input: test_data_dir contains test_windows.npy — float32 array of shape [2161, 96, 7], 2161 input windows each with 96 timesteps and 7 features, in column order (HUFL, HULL, MUFL, MULL, LUFL, LULL, OT). Values are raw (not normalized).
Output: JSON file with format {"predictions": [[[0.12, ...], ...], ...]}
        A nested list of shape [2161, 720, 7]: for each of the 2161 input windows, predict the next 720 timesteps for all 7 features. Values must be in the original (raw, not normalized) scale.

name: flip_aav
summary: Build a model to predict the fitness (transduction efficiency) of adeno-associated virus (AAV) capsid protein variants from amino acid sequences. This uses the AAV two_vs_many split from the FLIP benchmark.
description:
Build a model to predict the fitness (transduction efficiency) of adeno-associated virus (AAV) capsid protein variants from amino acid sequences. This uses the AAV two_vs_many split from the FLIP benchmark.

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - train.csv: CSV with columns "sequence" (amino acid string, ~735 chars) and "target" (float, fitness score). 28,626 training sequences.
  - val.csv: same format, 3,181 validation sequences (FLIP official validation flag).

Each sequence is the full AAV2 VP1 capsid protein (~735 amino acids, 1-letter code: A, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, S, T, V, W, Y). The "target" is a continuous fitness score (log-transformed transduction efficiency). Higher values indicate better viral transduction. Mutations are concentrated in positions 561-588 (a 28-AA region), but the full sequence is provided.
The two_vs_many split: training sequences have ≤2 mutations from wild type, test sequences have >2 mutations. This tests extrapolation from low-order to higher-order combinatorial mutants.

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>

Input: test_data_dir contains test.csv with column "sequence" only (no target). 50,776 test sequences.
Output: JSON file with format {"predictions": [0.42, -0.15, 1.23, ...]}
        One float fitness score per test sequence, in order.

name: habitat3
summary:
Train a social-navigation policy for the Boston Dynamics **Spot** robot in
Habitat 3.0 (Puig et al., 2023, https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.13724). The robot
shares a multi-room HSSD-200 home with a randomly-walking humanoid avatar
and must repeatedly *find* and *follow* the human while avoiding collisions.
description:
Train a social-navigation policy for the Boston Dynamics **Spot** robot in
Habitat 3.0 (Puig et al., 2023, https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.13724). The robot
shares a multi-room HSSD-200 home with a randomly-walking humanoid avatar
and must repeatedly *find* and *follow* the human while avoiding collisions.

Your goal: maximise `nav_seek_success` on the baked validation split: an
episode succeeds only if Spot
accumulates at least 500 steps at safe social distance (`1.0 <= distance <
2.0` meters) while facing the humanoid. The Habitat config names the
measurement `social_nav_seek_success`; `env.get_metrics()` exposes the
result as `nav_seek_success` — use that key verbatim.

## Multi-Agent Setup

The Habitat task config is `RearrangePddlSocialNavTask-v0`
(config file: `benchmark/multi_agent/hssd_spot_human_social_nav.yaml`):

  - **agent_0** = Spot (`spot` URDF, kinematic mode). YOUR policy controls
    this agent. Action: 2-D base velocity (longitudinal + angular).
  - **agent_1** = Humanoid avatar (`hab3_humanoid` from humanoid_data).
    Driven by an *oracle random-coord nav* — it walks to random reachable
    points in the scene by itself. You DO NOT train or control agent_1.

Episode terminates after `max_episode_steps = 750` simulation steps in the
shipped config. Success is `nav_seek_success`: Spot must maintain
`safe_dis_min = 1.0 m <= distance < safe_dis_max = 2.0 m` while facing the
human for `following_step_succ_threshold = 500` accumulated steps. Do NOT
confuse this with the easier distance-only `nav_to_pos_success`.

## Environment

Habitat-Sim 0.3.3 (headless EGL + Bullet) and habitat-lab 0.3.3 are
pre-installed in the conda env `habitat` (Python 3.9). The container
entrypoint sets up `/opt/habitat-lab-src/data → /rab_data/habitat3`
(image-baked, ~25 GB) and exposes the same tree at `/workspace/data`.

Available data (read these paths verbatim):

  - Scene dataset — H3 modified HSSD (use this one, see Quick-start):
      /opt/habitat-lab-src/data/hab3_bench_assets/hab3-hssd/
      Config: `hab3-hssd.scene_dataset_config.json` (already patched at
      build time to register the `hssd_ssd_map` semantic tag).
  - Original HSSD-200 (only used for semantic lexicon; you do NOT need
    to point to this directly):
      /opt/habitat-lab-src/data/scene_datasets/hssd-hab/
  - Episode dataset currently baked into this benchmark:
      /opt/habitat-lab-src/data/datasets/hssd/rearrange/{train,val}/social_rearrange.json.gz
      These are symlinks to `hab3_bench_assets/episode_datasets/medium_medium.json.gz`
      (scene-size & object-size both "medium"). Important: each
      `hab3_bench_assets/episode_datasets/<size>_<size>.json.gz` file has
      only 10 episodes, and train/val currently point to the same source.
      If this benchmark is later changed to official SocialNav episodes,
      switch the data UID/source to `hab3_episodes` instead of relying on
      `hab3_bench_assets`.
  - YCB object configs:
      /opt/habitat-lab-src/data/objects/ycb/
  - Spot URDF (agent_0, Boston Dynamics Spot quadruped):
      /opt/habitat-lab-src/data/robots/hab_spot_arm/
  - Humanoid avatars (agent_1, SMPL-X-style female motion data):
      /opt/habitat-lab-src/data/humanoids/humanoid_data/

Habitat resolves all `data/...` paths **relative to cwd**. You MUST call
`os.chdir('/opt/habitat-lab-src')` BEFORE `import habitat` in EVERY script
(train.py, predict.py, any test scripts). The eval container is read-only
on `/workspace`, so do NOT chdir into `/workspace`.

## Observation Space (Spot's POV — what your policy gets at each step)

The full env returns a flat dict (multi-agent obs are namespaced by
`agent_X_`). For Spot's policy you only need agent_0_* keys:

  - `agent_0_head_rgb`                     : uint8   (256, 256, 3)
  - `agent_0_head_depth`                   : float32 (256, 256, 1)
  - `agent_0_articulated_agent_arm_rgb`    : uint8   (480, 640, 3)
  - `agent_0_articulated_agent_arm_depth`  : float32 (240, 228, 1)
  - `agent_0_head_stereo_left_depth`       : float32 (212, 120, 1)
  - `agent_0_head_stereo_right_depth`      : float32 (212, 120, 1)
  - `agent_0_spot_head_stereo_depth_sensor`: float32 (240, 228, 1)
  - `agent_0_humanoid_detector_sensor`     : float32 (1,) binary "human in view"
                                              (config sets return_image=False)
  - `agent_0_goal_to_agent_gps_compass`    : float32 (2,) [dist, angle] to human
  - `agent_0_other_agent_gps`              : float32 (2,) relative human position
  - `agent_0_localization_sensor`          : float32 (4,) Spot pose proxy

agent_1_* keys are humanoid-internal sensors — your policy does NOT need
them (the oracle nav handles agent_1's actions).

## Action Space

Multi-agent actions are passed as a *flat dict*. Spot's policy outputs
ONLY agent_0's base velocity; agent_1's oracle nav is invoked
automatically by the env. Pass agent_1's no-op explicitly each step:

    action = {
        "action": ("agent_0_base_velocity", "agent_1_oracle_nav_randcoord_action"),
        "action_args": {
            # agent_0: Spot base velocity (2-D, lateral disabled)
            "agent_0_base_vel": np.array([lin, ang], dtype=np.float32),  # ∈ [-1, 1]^2
            # agent_1: oracle picks its own coord, just trigger the action
            "agent_1_oracle_nav_randcoord_action": np.array([1.0], dtype=np.float32),
        },
    }

- `agent_0_base_vel[0]` = forward speed   (positive = forward, negative = backward)
- `agent_0_base_vel[1]` = angular speed   (positive = turn left)

Lateral motion is disabled (`enable_lateral_move: False`).

## Quick-start Example

    import os, numpy as np
    os.chdir('/opt/habitat-lab-src')

    import habitat
    from habitat.config.default import get_config
    from habitat.config import read_write

    cfg_path = (
        "/opt/habitat-lab-src/habitat-lab/habitat/config/"
        "benchmark/multi_agent/hssd_spot_human_social_nav.yaml"
    )
    config = get_config(cfg_path)
    with read_write(config):
        config.habitat.simulator.scene_dataset = (
            "/opt/habitat-lab-src/data/hab3_bench_assets/hab3-hssd/"
            "hab3-hssd.scene_dataset_config.json"
        )
        config.habitat.dataset.scenes_dir = "/opt/habitat-lab-src/data/scene_datasets"

    with habitat.Env(config=config) as env:
        obs = env.reset()
        # Inspect what's actually there:
        print({k: getattr(v, "shape", v) for k, v in obs.items()})

        for step in range(50):
            action = {
                "action": ("agent_0_base_velocity",
                           "agent_1_oracle_nav_randcoord_action"),
                "action_args": {
                    "agent_0_base_vel": np.random.uniform(-1, 1, 2).astype(np.float32),
                    "agent_1_oracle_nav_randcoord_action":
                        np.array([1.0], dtype=np.float32),
                },
            }
            obs = env.step(action)
            if env.episode_over:
                break
        metrics = env.get_metrics()
        # Real metric keys returned by env.get_metrics() in this image.
        # Use these EXACT names — `social_nav_seek_success` does NOT exist
        # as a top-level metric in habitat-lab 0.3.3.
        print("seek_success:", metrics["nav_seek_success"])
        print("reward:      ", metrics["social_nav_reward"])
        print("stats:       ", metrics["social_nav_stats"])

## Metrics (returned by `env.get_metrics()` in this image)

Top-level keys:
  - `nav_seek_success` (float, 0/1): **PRIMARY METRIC**. 1 iff Spot
    accumulates ≥500 steps with `1.0 <= distance_to_human < 2.0` AND facing
    the human. Reported only at episode end (`env.episode_over == True`).
  - `social_nav_reward` (float): Habitat's shaped social-nav measure. If you
    need a per-step scalar reward in a raw `habitat.Env` loop, use the
    adjacent-step difference `metrics_t["social_nav_reward"] -
    metrics_{t-1}["social_nav_reward"]`, or use Habitat's gym wrapper step
    reward. Do not treat the final cumulative/measurement value as a
    ready-made per-step training reward.
  - `nav_to_pos_success` (float, 0/1): distance-based success.
  - `dist_to_goal` (float): meters to closest goal point.
  - `robot_collisions` (dict): per-step / cumulative collision counts.
  - `did_collide` (bool): collision flag for the current step.
  - `articulated_agent_force` (float): magnitude of contact forces.
  - `num_steps` (int): elapsed steps in this episode.

`social_nav_stats` (dict, also returned at episode end):
  - `has_found_human` (bool)
  - `found_human_rate_over_epi` (float, 0–1)
  - `found_human_rate_after_encounter_over_epi` (float, 0–1)
  - `avg_robot_to_human_dis_over_epi` (float, m)
  - `avg_robot_to_human_after_encounter_dis_over_epi` (float, m)
  - `first_encounter_spl` (float, 0–1; usually 0 here because shortest-path
    computation is disabled in the current config)
  - `frist_ecnounter_steps` (int) — note habitat's typo "frist"
  - `frist_ecnounter_steps_ratio` (float, 0–1)
  - `follow_human_steps_after_frist_encounter` (int)
  - `follow_human_steps_ratio_after_frist_encounter` (float, 0–1; note the
    typo in the key. In the current config this is typically 0.0 because
    shortest-path-related fields are disabled, so it is only a diagnostic,
    not a useful secondary leaderboard metric.)
  - `backup_ratio` (float, 0–1) — % of steps moving backwards
  - `yield_ratio` (float, 0–1) — % of steps yielding to human

Keys that do NOT exist (despite some H3 docs/papers): `social_nav_seek_success`,
`pddl_success`, `nav_to_pos_succ` (note: real key is `nav_to_pos_success`),
`follow_human_steps_after_frame_view`, `total_distance_to_human`. Do NOT
reference them — your code will KeyError.

## Training

- On-policy (or off-policy) RL via simulator interaction. NO static dataset
  to load — every training step interacts with the live MuJoCo-style sim.
- One env step ≈ 30–50 ms on a single GPU (depth rendering + 2-agent sim
  + physics). 100k env steps ≈ 50–80 minutes per process.
- The episode dataset
  `data/datasets/hssd/rearrange/train/social_rearrange.json.gz` is currently
  a symlink to `hab3_bench_assets/episode_datasets/medium_medium.json.gz`,
  which contains only 10 episodes. Train and val are currently same-source
  splits. If you need official SocialNav train/val/test episodes, this task
  should be migrated to the `hab3_episodes` data source.

IMPORTANT — Training monitoring:
- Each `env.step()` is ~40 ms (NOT ~1 ms). 100 PPO updates × 128 steps
  ≈ 9 min just for stepping. Plan accordingly.
- Implement early stopping: if mean_reward and seek_success are both flat
  after 100+ updates, STOP and try different hparams / reward shaping /
  architecture. Do NOT let a non-converging run consume all 10 hours.
- Print progress (reward, seek_success, elapsed) every 10–20 updates.
  Watch stdout/stderr — repeated `KeyError` / `Env step error` means
  your action dict shape is wrong; STOP and fix immediately.

## Predict Script Contract

Harness invocation (always with absolute paths):

    python predict.py --data_path <abs_dir> --checkpoint <abs_ckpt_dir> --output <abs_json>

Note: `--data_path` exists only to satisfy FARBench's cross-task contract;
there is no static test set for this RL task. **Ignore `--data_path`**
and hardcode the eval config below in your predict.py.

predict.py MUST:
  1. `os.path.abspath()` every CLI arg, THEN
     `os.chdir('/opt/habitat-lab-src')`, THEN `import habitat`. (Skipping
     step 1 makes any later `open()` resolve against `/opt/habitat-lab-src`
     — see "PATH FOOTGUN" in agent_hints.)
  2. Hardcode the eval params:
         SPLIT = "val"; NUM_EPISODES = 50; SEED = 42
    (Episodes come from the baked `val` symlink to
    `hab3_bench_assets/episode_datasets/medium_medium.json.gz`. No external
    test data needed. There are only 10 unique episode definitions in that
    file, so 50 eval episodes means repeated sampling under the simulator
    seed, not 50 unique official SocialNav episodes.)
  3. Load `torch.load(os.path.join(args.checkpoint, "model.pt"))`. Save
     the same filename in train.py.
  4. Build env as in Quick-start (with the two `read_write` overrides),
     run `NUM_EPISODES` episodes from `SPLIT`, write JSON to `<args.output>`.

Output JSON format (REQUIRED — exact key names; the evaluator re-aggregates
from `per_episode` regardless of top-level values). `safe_follow_steps` is
optional but strongly recommended: the evaluator uses it to recompute
`nav_seek_success = (safe_follow_steps >= 500)` under the true Habitat3
definition. If you do not emit it, the evaluator falls back to Habitat's own
episode-end `metrics["nav_seek_success"]`.

    {
        "primary_metric": "nav_seek_success",
        "nav_seek_success":         0.42,
        "social_nav_reward":        -3.1,
        "found_human_rate_over_epi": 0.55,
        "safe_follow_steps":         520,
        "avg_robot_to_human_dis_over_epi": 1.85,
        "nav_to_pos_success":        0.30,
        "per_episode": [
            {
                "episode_id": "0",
                "nav_seek_success":   1,
                "social_nav_reward":  4.2,
                "found_human_rate_over_epi": 0.7,
                "safe_follow_steps": 520,
                "avg_robot_to_human_dis_over_epi": 1.5,
                "nav_to_pos_success": 1
            },
            ...
        ],
        "total_episodes": 50
    }

All aggregate fields MUST be the mean of `per_episode` values. The evaluator
re-computes them from `per_episode`, and for `nav_seek_success` it prefers
`safe_follow_steps >= 500` over a pre-computed success flag when present.

## Constraints

  - Model parameter count ≤ 1B.
  - Training is on-policy simulator interaction; there is no static dataset
    to fine-tune on. Log progress every 50 updates.
  - The Habitat config is READ-ONLY (`OmegaConf` frozen struct). Wrap any
    modification in `from habitat.config import read_write; with read_write(config): ...`
    — this task requires the two dataset-path overrides shown in Quick-start.

## Reference numbers from the literature

You have NO network access during the run, so these published baselines
are the only SOTA datapoints you'll get. Habitat 3.0 (Puig et al.,
ICLR 2024) reports the following for the Spot Social-Nav setup; "S" =
Finding Success, "SPS" = Success-weighted Path Steps, "F" = Following
Rate (1-2 m & facing), "CR" = Collision Rate:

    Method                                         S       SPS     F       CR
    ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    Heuristic Expert (map + shortest path)         1.00    0.97    0.51    0.52
    End-to-end RL DDPPO (8-GPU × 100M+ env steps)  0.97    0.65    0.44    0.51
    SDA (Scofano et al., CVPR-W'24)                0.91    0.45    0.39    —
    COMM (CommNav, NeurIPS'25 extension)           0.78    —       0.51    —

Two non-obvious takeaways relevant to this benchmark's compute budget:
 • The map+planner Heuristic Expert OUTPERFORMS end-to-end RL on this
   benchmark. A planner-based or heuristic controller is a legitimate
   research direction here, not a fallback.
 • Paper RL was trained with ~100M env steps on 8 GPUs — orders of
   magnitude beyond a 10-hour single-machine budget. Training PPO from
   scratch is unlikely to converge in your budget; consider using or
   imitating a classical controller before / instead of RL.

## Tooling already installed that may be useful

habitat-lab 0.3.3 ships a navmesh-based geodesic path planner at
    `habitat.tasks.nav.shortest_path_follower.ShortestPathFollower`
(the same class the paper's Heuristic Expert is built on). It was
designed for single-agent NavTask, so reusing it here requires some
glue: the goal is a moving humanoid, and the follower returns discrete
navigation actions while agent_0's action space is continuous
base-velocity. Read its source if you choose to use it (~80 lines).

## Execution Priority

  1. First, write a *short* test script (< 30 lines) that creates the
     env, prints `obs` keys/shapes and `env.action_space` structure,
     runs 5 random-action steps, prints `env.get_metrics()`. This is
     your ONE exploration step.
  2. Decide on an approach (heuristic / planner-based / imitation /
     RL — see literature numbers above) and write `train.py` +
     `predict.py`. Submit eval EARLY to measure runtime and confirm
     the harness contract works end-to-end.
  3. Inspect eval results, training curves, and elapsed time. Decide:
     - Continue / extend the current approach if metrics are improving.
     - Change approach (architecture, reward shaping, hparams) if not.
  4. Iterate; submit best checkpoint before the time budget expires.

name: humanoidbench
summary:
Train an online RL policy on 10 representative HumanoidBench tasks and maximise
`success_rate` averaged over held-out evaluation episodes.
description:
Train an online RL policy on 10 representative HumanoidBench tasks and maximise
`success_rate` averaged over held-out evaluation episodes.

HumanoidBench (https://humanoid-bench.github.io/) provides 27 whole-body
humanoid control tasks with the Unitree H1 robot (plus Shadow Hands for
manipulation), simulated in MuJoCo. We select a diverse subset of 10 tasks
covering locomotion, balance and manipulation so the agent must learn
transferable skills, not overfit a single task.

## Goal

Maximise the overall `success_rate` across the 10 evaluation tasks,
each rolled out for a fixed set of episodes with deterministic seeds
(see `eval_config.json`).

## Evaluation tasks (10)

All 10 use the **`h1hand`** robot (Unitree H1 19-dof body + 2x Shadow Hand
21-dof each). action_space is identical across all 10 tasks:
**Box(-1, 1, (61,), float32)** — so a single multi-task policy can output
one 61-D action vector for every task. observation_space differs per task:

| task_id                       | obs_dim | max_steps | success_bar | info["success"]? |
|-------------------------------|--------:|----------:|------------:|:----------------:|
| h1hand-walk-v0                |     151 |      1000 |         700 |        no        |
| h1hand-stand-v0               |     151 |      1000 |         800 |        no        |
| h1hand-run-v0                 |     151 |      1000 |         700 |        no        |
| h1hand-sit_simple-v0          |     151 |      1000 |         750 |        no        |
| h1hand-balance_simple-v0      |     164 |      1000 |         800 |        no        |
| h1hand-reach-v0               |     157 |      1000 |       12000 |        no        |
| h1hand-push-v0                |     163 |       500 |         700 |        YES       |
| h1hand-pole-v0                |     151 |      1000 |         700 |        no        |
| h1hand-door-v0                |     155 |      1000 |         600 |        no        |
| h1hand-package-v0             |     173 |      1000 |        1500 |        YES       |

These numbers are not paper-quoted — they were measured live inside this
image (see Dockerfile sanity check). They are the **single source of
truth** for action_space, observation_space, episode length, and success
threshold; the same table is also baked into eval_config.json so
predict.py can read it programmatically.

## Success definition (READ THIS — agents commonly get it wrong)

Official HumanoidBench protocol: **`episode_success = (sum(rewards) >=
task.success_bar)`**. The `success_bar` per task is in the table above and
in `eval_config.json`. Only `h1hand-push-v0` and `h1hand-package-v0`
ALSO emit `info["success"]` from `env.step()`; for the OTHER 8 tasks
there is NO `success` key in `info`, so `info.get("success", 0)` returns
0 forever and your `success_rate` will silently be 0/200.

predict.py MUST:
  - sum the per-step reward into `episode_total_reward`, and
  - emit it as `per_episode[i]["reward"]` (the framework name).
The evaluator then recomputes `success = (reward >= success_bar)` server
side using the bars in `eval_config.json`. predict.py MAY also report a
per-episode `success` field; the evaluator ignores it when `reward` and
`success_bar` are both available (i.e. server-side recomputation wins).

## Pre-installed environment

Everything is ready inside the container:
  - MuJoCo >= 3.1 (no manual activation key needed)
  - humanoid-bench repo at /opt/humanoid-bench (pip installed in editable mode)
  - dm_control, gymnasium
  - PyTorch, stable-baselines3, tensorboard
  - Registered Gymnasium ids: `h1hand-<task>-v0` (61-dim action, the 10
    eval tasks use this). `h1-<task>-v0` (no-hand variant, 19-dim action)
    is ALSO registered but is NOT in the eval set — training on the no-hand
    variant and submitting it as `h1hand-walk-v0` will fail with
    `ValueError: Cannot feed value of shape (19,) for Tensor of shape (61,)`.

## Training

Training is fully ONLINE RL (no static dataset). The agent creates
Gymnasium envs and interacts with the MuJoCo simulator.

    import gymnasium as gym
    import humanoid_bench  # registers the envs
    env = gym.make("h1hand-walk-v0")
    obs, info = env.reset(seed=0)
    for _ in range(1000):
        action = env.action_space.sample()   # replace with policy
        obs, reward, terminated, truncated, info = env.step(action)
        if terminated or truncated:
            break
    success = float(info.get("success", 0.0))

You MAY train on any subset of the 10 eval tasks, or on a multi-task
policy that covers all 10. Training data = your own rollouts.

## Predict script contract

You MUST create `predict.py` in /workspace that:
  1. Loads your trained policy from `--checkpoint` directory.
  2. Reads the eval protocol from `<data_path>/eval_config.json` which
     specifies the list of tasks and, per task: `obs_dim`, `success_bar`,
     `max_episode_steps`, `info_has_success`, `episodes`, `seeds`.
  3. For each (task, episode) pair, rolls out the policy in the real
     HumanoidBench environment, sums per-step rewards into
     `episode_total_reward`, and records that as the per-episode `reward`.
  4. Writes a JSON results file to `--output`.

Invocation:
  python predict.py --data_path <data_path> --checkpoint <ckpt_dir> --output <output.json>

Required output format (the evaluator recomputes success_rate server side
from `per_episode` using the success_bar in eval_config.json — `reward`
here means episode-total cumulative reward, NOT mean step reward):

  {
    "total_episodes": 200,
    "per_episode": [
      {"task_id": "h1hand-walk-v0", "episode_id": 0, "seed": 0, "reward": 355.2},
      ...
    ]
  }

Optional but useful (the evaluator ignores them when present, but they
show up in the metrics dict for debugging):
  {
    "success_rate": 0.42,
    "mean_reward":  120.3,
    "per_task":     {"h1hand-walk-v0": {"success_rate": 0.8, "mean_reward": 350.1, "n_episodes": 20}, ...},
    "per_episode":  [{... "success": 1, ...}, ...]
  }

Whatever you report at the top level is REPLACED by the evaluator's
recomputation from `per_episode` × `success_bar`. So the *only* thing you
must get right is `per_episode[i]["reward"]` (cumulative episode reward).

## Execution priority

- Start with a very short test script (<= 20 lines): `gym.make("h1hand-walk-v0")`,
  reset, 5 random steps, print obs shape / reward / info keys. Only one short
  exploration step — do NOT spend iterations debugging MuJoCo / GPU / paths.
- Pick ONE task first (e.g. `h1hand-walk-v0`), train a minimal PPO/SAC baseline,
  submit eval. Iterate on reward shaping / hyperparams.
- Then scale to multi-task training covering all 10.
- Each env.step() is cheap compared to Habitat (~1–3 ms), so you can afford
  a few million env steps per task within the time budget.

## Constraints

- Policy parameter count <= 100M (keep it tractable; HumanoidBench policies
  are typically < 5M).
- Network access: false.
- Training data = your own online rollouts. No external humanoid datasets.

name: iwildcam_wilds
summary: Build a 182-class wildlife species classifier from camera-trap images that generalizes to camera-trap locations NEVER seen during training (spatial OOD domain generalization). Evaluation metric is macro-F1 restricted to the set of classes that appear in the OOD test ground-truth (matches wilds.common.metrics.all_metrics.F1 with labels=torch.unique(y_true) — critical because many species are train-only or val-only; sklearn's default would penalize unseen classes and make our numbers incomparable to published baselines). Splits follow WILDS v2.0 protocol bit-for-bit (Koh et al., ICML 2021 / v2.0 CSV with split IDs 0=train, 1=val, 2=test, 3=id_val, 4=id_test; FARBench uses 0/1/2, drops id_val/id_test per leaderboard convention). Sample counts (WILDS published): train = 129,809 images from 243 locations (12 countries), val = 14,961 from 32 OOD locations, test = 42,791 from 48 OOD locations — train/val/test locations are strictly disjoint. Class distribution is extremely long-tailed: popular classes (empty frame, cattle, deer) have tens of thousands of samples while many rare species have fewer than 100 — this is why the benchmark uses macro-F1, not accuracy.
description:
Build a 182-class wildlife species classifier from camera-trap images that generalizes to camera-trap locations NEVER seen during training (spatial OOD domain generalization). Evaluation metric is macro-F1 restricted to the set of classes that appear in the OOD test ground-truth (matches wilds.common.metrics.all_metrics.F1 with labels=torch.unique(y_true) — critical because many species are train-only or val-only; sklearn's default would penalize unseen classes and make our numbers incomparable to published baselines). Splits follow WILDS v2.0 protocol bit-for-bit (Koh et al., ICML 2021 / v2.0 CSV with split IDs 0=train, 1=val, 2=test, 3=id_val, 4=id_test; FARBench uses 0/1/2, drops id_val/id_test per leaderboard convention). Sample counts (WILDS published): train = 129,809 images from 243 locations (12 countries), val = 14,961 from 32 OOD locations, test = 42,791 from 48 OOD locations — train/val/test locations are strictly disjoint. Class distribution is extremely long-tailed: popular classes (empty frame, cattle, deer) have tens of thousands of samples while many rare species have fewer than 100 — this is why the benchmark uses macro-F1, not accuracy.

## Data Format

Training data at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container, ~12 GB on disk):
  - train.txt              : 129,809 lines of "<filename>.jpg {label}" (space-separated, label ∈ [0, 181], filenames whitespace-free)
  - val.txt                : 14,961 lines, same format (OOD val, 32 held-out locations)
  - location_labels_train.txt : 129,809 lines, one integer per line (aligned by line index to train.txt); location IDs are in [0, 242] (remapped to be contiguous)
  - location_labels_val.txt   : 14,961 lines, same format as above (location IDs for val; the 32 val locations are disjoint from the 243 train locations, i.e. IDs here do NOT overlap with train's 0-242)
  - class_names.txt        : 182 lines, one canonical species name per line (line index == class index 0-181; names loaded from categories.csv shipped with WILDS v2.0 bundle)
  - images/                : flat directory of .jpg files (real files, ~145K total = train + val combined); fully self-contained, no external dependencies

Test data at $FARBENCH_TEST_DATA_DIR (/data in the eval container), given to predict.py:
  - test.txt               : 42,791 lines of "<filename>.jpg" ONLY — no label column
  - images/                : flat directory with the test .jpg files
  (test_labels.txt also lives in this directory; it is used by the evaluator. predict.py MUST only read test.txt and images/ — reading test_labels.txt is considered cheating.)

Image spec:
  - Format:     JPEG, RGB, 3 channels, 8-bit unsigned (pixel range 0-255)
  - Size:       VARIABLE — most images are 1024x747 or similar "landscape" aspect; sizes range roughly 100 px to 2000 px per side. Not pre-resized. Agent must resize or crop in its own transform pipeline.
  - Filename:   the basename of the original iWildCam file (e.g. "8a37a24a-21bc-11ea-a13a-137349068a90.jpg"); treat as an opaque identifier — do NOT try to parse metadata from the filename.
  - Content:    RGB camera-trap photos captured by motion sensors in various biomes — savannah, jungle, forest, etc. Lighting varies (daytime color, nighttime IR/grayscale-like), occlusion varies, motion blur common.
  - Normalization: pixel values are raw uint8 (0-255), NOT pre-normalized. Apply your model's own mean/std after loading.

Label encoding:
  - Labels are integers in [0, 181] covering 182 species categories.
  - class 0 = "empty" (no animal, triggered motion sensor by wind / foliage) — this is a real class in iWildCam and is included in macro-F1.
  - Class distribution is HEAVILY long-tailed:
      * 5-10 classes have > 5,000 training samples each (empty, common ungulates, livestock)
      * ~50 classes have 100-5,000 samples (common mammals, birds)
      * 100+ classes have fewer than 100 samples (rare species) — many have < 20 samples
      * Many classes present in train are absent from val or test, and vice-versa. The evaluator restricts macro-F1 to classes that appear in y_true, so missing-from-test classes do not contribute.
  - See class_names.txt for canonical names (may be generic "species_N" if categories.csv was unavailable during preparation — prepare.py prints a warning in that case).

Location encoding:
  - 243 training locations + 32 OOD val locations + 48 OOD test locations = 323 total, location IDs in [0, 322] remapped to be contiguous in metadata.csv (column: location_remapped).
  - location_labels_{train,val}.txt expose the remapped IDs for supervised training / domain-aware techniques. test locations are NOT revealed to the agent (no test location file is visible under $FARBENCH_TEST_DATA_DIR).
  - Using location labels during training is optional; the predict.py contract does not require or forbid it.

Why this task is hard (context only, no method implied):
  - Spatial OOD shift: backgrounds, vegetation, camera angle, and species composition differ dramatically across the 48 test locations; many test locations are in biomes underrepresented or absent in training.
  - Long-tail + macro-F1: the metric weights every present-in-test class equally, so correctly recognizing rare species matters as much as common ones — a model that scores well on only common classes will have a low macro-F1.
  - Visual variation within a class: the same species looks very different under daytime color vs. nighttime IR imagery, in dense foliage vs. open plains.

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>

Input: <test_data_dir> contains test.txt (one "<filename>.jpg" per line, NO labels) and images/ (the .jpg files referenced by test.txt).
Output: JSON file at <output.json> with format {"predictions": [42, 0, 181, 5, ...]}
        One predicted class index (int in [0, 181]) per test sample, in the SAME order as test.txt lines. Length MUST equal len(test.txt) = 42,791.

name: metrla_traffic
summary: Build a spatiotemporal graph model to predict traffic speeds on 207 Los Angeles highway sensors. Given the past 12 timesteps (1 hour at 5-min intervals), predict the next 12 timesteps (1 hour) for all 207 sensors.
description:
Build a spatiotemporal graph model to predict traffic speeds on 207 Los Angeles highway sensors. Given the past 12 timesteps (1 hour at 5-min intervals), predict the next 12 timesteps (1 hour) for all 207 sensors.

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - train.npy: float32 array [23,990, 207] — 23,990 consecutive 5-minute traffic speed readings (mph) for 207 sensors (~83 days)
  - val.npy: float32 array [3,427, 207] — 3,427 consecutive timesteps (~12 days)
  - adj_mx.npy: float32 array [207, 207] — weighted adjacency matrix (Gaussian kernel on road-network distances, threshold=0.1)

Speed values are in miles per hour (raw, not normalized). Missing values have been linearly interpolated.
The adjacency matrix encodes spatial proximity: W_ij = exp(-d^2/sigma^2) if above threshold, else 0.
To create training samples, use a sliding window: input_len=12, pred_len=12, stride=1. From T consecutive timesteps, extract (T - 23) pairs of (x[t:t+12], y[t+12:t+24]).

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>

Input: test_data_dir contains:
  - test_x.npy: float32 [6,832, 12, 207] — 6,832 test input windows (12 steps × 207 sensors)
  - adj_mx.npy: float32 [207, 207] — same adjacency matrix as training
Output: Two things:
  1. Save predictions as predictions.npy (float32 [6832, 12, 207]) in a directory next to the output JSON
  2. Write JSON: {"predictions_dir": "<absolute_path_to_directory>/"}
  Predictions must be in raw mph scale (not normalized).

name: minigrid
summary:
Train an RL agent to solve navigation and puzzle tasks in Minigrid
grid-world environments.
description:
Train an RL agent to solve navigation and puzzle tasks in Minigrid
grid-world environments.

Your goal: train an RL policy that maximises success rate (primary metric)
across multiple Minigrid environments. You have complete research freedom —
choose any RL algorithm, network architecture, hyperparameters, and training
strategies (e.g., multi-task, curriculum learning, separate policies).

## Environment

Minigrid is a lightweight Gymnasium-based library of discrete grid-world
environments. The agent (a triangle) navigates 2D grids with walls, doors,
keys, lava, and other obstacles.

Install: `pip install minigrid` (network access is enabled).

Observation space (dict):
  - "image":     uint8 (7, 7, 3) — partial egocentric grid view.
                 Each cell encodes (OBJECT_IDX, COLOR_IDX, STATE).
  - "direction": Discrete(4) — agent facing direction.
  - "mission":   str — natural language goal description.

Action space: Discrete(7)
  0=turn left, 1=turn right, 2=move forward,
  3=pickup, 4=drop, 5=toggle (open door), 6=done.

Reward: positive on success (higher for faster completion);
0 on failure (timeout / lava).

## Training Environments (10 environments)

Use these environments for training:
  - MiniGrid-DoorKey-8x8-v0
  - MiniGrid-FourRooms-v0
  - MiniGrid-KeyCorridorS3R3-v0
  - MiniGrid-LockedRoom-v0
  - MiniGrid-Dynamic-Obstacles-8x8-v0
  - MiniGrid-LavaCrossingS9N2-v0
  - MiniGrid-UnlockPickup-v0
  - MiniGrid-MultiRoom-N4-S5-v0
  - MiniGrid-LavaGapS7-v0
  - MiniGrid-DistShift1-v0

## Evaluation Environments (10 environments)

Evaluation uses the exact same environments as training (to measure
in-distribution generalisation via procedural generation using seeds 0-49).

Eval environments:
  - MiniGrid-DoorKey-8x8-v0
  - MiniGrid-FourRooms-v0
  - MiniGrid-KeyCorridorS3R3-v0
  - MiniGrid-LockedRoom-v0
  - MiniGrid-Dynamic-Obstacles-8x8-v0
  - MiniGrid-LavaCrossingS9N2-v0
  - MiniGrid-UnlockPickup-v0
  - MiniGrid-MultiRoom-N4-S5-v0
  - MiniGrid-LavaGapS7-v0
  - MiniGrid-DistShift1-v0

Each eval environment runs 50 episodes (seeds 0-49) → 500 episodes total.

## Quick-start Example

  import gymnasium as gym
  import minigrid

  env = gym.make("MiniGrid-Empty-8x8-v0", render_mode="rgb_array")
  obs, info = env.reset(seed=0)
  for step in range(env.max_steps):
      action = policy.predict(obs)  # your RL policy
      obs, reward, terminated, truncated, info = env.step(action)
      if terminated or truncated:
          break

## Predict Script Contract

You MUST create predict.py in /workspace that:
  1. Loads your trained policy from --checkpoint directory.
  2. Runs the 10 evaluation environments (50 episodes each, seeds 0-49).
  3. Writes a JSON results file to --output.

Invocation:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <ckpt_dir> --output <output.json>

Output JSON format (required):
  {
    "success_rate": 0.85,
    "mean_reward": 0.72,
    "per_env": {
      "MiniGrid-DoorKey-8x8-v0":            {"mean_reward": 0.45, "success_rate": 0.58},
      "MiniGrid-FourRooms-v0":              {"mean_reward": 0.40, "success_rate": 0.52},
      "MiniGrid-KeyCorridorS3R3-v0":        {"mean_reward": 0.42, "success_rate": 0.55},
      "MiniGrid-LockedRoom-v0":             {"mean_reward": 0.38, "success_rate": 0.48},
      "MiniGrid-Dynamic-Obstacles-8x8-v0":  {"mean_reward": 0.30, "success_rate": 0.40},
      "MiniGrid-LavaCrossingS9N2-v0":       {"mean_reward": 0.35, "success_rate": 0.45},
      "MiniGrid-UnlockPickup-v0":           {"mean_reward": 0.50, "success_rate": 0.60},
      "MiniGrid-MultiRoom-N4-S5-v0":        {"mean_reward": 0.48, "success_rate": 0.56},
      "MiniGrid-LavaGapS7-v0":              {"mean_reward": 0.32, "success_rate": 0.42},
      "MiniGrid-DistShift1-v0":             {"mean_reward": 0.44, "success_rate": 0.54}
    },
    "total_episodes": 500
  }

success_rate is the fraction of episodes with reward > 0 (primary metric).
