mean_reward is the average reward across all 500 episodes.
per_env contains the same metrics broken down by environment.

## Execution Priority

Submit evaluation after each training run to track progress.
Iterate based on eval results — do not train multiple times without evaluating.

## Constraints

- Network architecture is free but total parameters must be ≤ 10M.
- You may use any RL algorithm and observation wrappers as needed.

name: objaverse_3dgen
summary:
Single-image 3D generation: given one input view of an object, reconstruct a 3D
representation of the object and render it from multiple target camera poses.
Evaluation compares rendered images against ground truth using LPIPS, PSNR, and SSIM.
description:
Single-image 3D generation: given one input view of an object, reconstruct a 3D
representation of the object and render it from multiple target camera poses.
Evaluation compares rendered images against ground truth using LPIPS, PSNR, and SSIM.

Data source: Arb-Objaverse (curated Objaverse subset with pre-rendered multi-view images).
1000 objects split into 800 train / 100 val / 100 test.
Each object has 12 viewpoints with known camera parameters (azimuth, elevation, distance).
The agent receives 1 input view and must generate the remaining 11 target views.

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - meta.json: metadata with fields:
      "n_objects_train": 800, "n_objects_val": 100, "n_objects_test": 100,
      "n_views": 12, "image_size": 256,
      "source": "arb_objaverse"
  - train/<object_uid>/
      input.png        — single input view (view index 0), 256x256 RGBA PNG
      target_views/
        view_01.png ... view_11.png   — 11 target views, 256x256 RGBA PNG
      cameras.json     — camera parameters for all 12 views:
        [{"view_id": 0, "azimuth": ..., "elevation": ..., "distance": ...}, ...]
  - val/<object_uid>/   — same structure, 100 objects for validation

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>

Input: --data_path is a DIRECTORY containing:
         <object_uid>/input.png       — single input view per object
         <object_uid>/cameras.json    — target camera poses
       For each object, generate 11 novel views matching the target camera poses.
Output: Two things:
  1. A directory of rendered images saved next to the output JSON.
     Directory name = output filename (without .json) + "_renders/".
     Example: if --output is /results/eval_predictions.json,
     save renders to /results/eval_predictions_renders/<object_uid>/view_01.png, ..., view_11.png
     Each PNG is an RGBA uint8 image at 256x256 resolution.
  2. The JSON file at --output with format: {"predictions_dir": "/results/eval_predictions_renders/"}

name: ogbg_molpcba
summary: Build a multi-task binary classification model for 128 bioassay activity predictions on molecular graphs (ogbg-molpcba from Open Graph Benchmark).
description:
Build a multi-task binary classification model for 128 bioassay activity predictions on molecular graphs (ogbg-molpcba from Open Graph Benchmark).

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - train.pt: dict with keys:
      "node_features": list of 350,343 tensors, each shape [num_atoms, 9] (int64, 9-dim atom features)
      "edge_index": list of 350,343 tensors, each shape [2, num_bonds] (int64, COO edge indices)
      "edge_attr": list of 350,343 tensors, each shape [num_bonds, 3] (int64, 3-dim bond features)
      "labels": list of 350,343 tensors, each shape [128] (float32, binary labels with NaN for missing)
  - val.pt: same format, 43,793 graphs

Each molecule is a graph: nodes are atoms (9 features: atomic number, chirality, degree, formal charge,
num Hs, radical electrons, hybridization, aromaticity, ring membership), edges are bonds (3 features:
bond type, stereochemistry, is_conjugated). Labels are 128 binary bioassay outcomes (0/1) with NaN
for missing values. Only ~1.4% of labeled entries are positive (extreme class imbalance).
When computing loss, mask out NaN entries (NaN != NaN trick: is_labeled = y == y).

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>

Input: test_data_dir contains test.pt with keys "node_features", "edge_index", "edge_attr" (no labels).
Output: JSON file with format {"predictions": [[0.12, null, 0.87, ...], [0.03, 0.95, null, ...], ...]}
        One list of 128 float probabilities per molecule, in order. Use null for tasks where prediction
        is not available (though predicting all 128 is strongly recommended).

name: qlib_stock
summary: Train a stock return prediction model on CSI300 (沪深300) daily data using the Alpha158 feature set from Microsoft Qlib. Maximise IC (Information Coefficient) between predicted and actual future returns on the held-out test period.
description:
Train a stock return prediction model on CSI300 (沪深300) daily data using the Alpha158 feature set from Microsoft Qlib. Maximise IC (Information Coefficient) between predicted and actual future returns on the held-out test period.

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - meta.json — metadata:
      "universe": "csi300", "features": "Alpha158",
      "train_period": ["2008-01-01", "2020-12-31"],
      "valid_period": ["2021-01-01", "2021-12-31"],
      "label": "Ref($close, -1) / $close - 1" (next-day return)
  - qlib_data/ — Qlib binary data directory (set provider_uri to this path)

Given daily stock features (Alpha158: 158 technical indicators including price/volume ratios,
moving averages, volatility, etc.), predict the next-day return for each stock in the CSI300
universe. The model is evaluated via cross-sectional IC: at each trading day, compute the
Pearson correlation between predicted scores and actual returns across all stocks.

## Predict Script Contract
You MUST create predict.py in /workspace that:
  1. Loads your trained model from --checkpoint directory.
  2. Generates predictions for the test period (2022-01-01 to 2023-12-31).
  3. Writes a JSON results file to --output.

Invocation:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <ckpt_dir> --output <output.json>

Input: --data_path contains:
  eval_config.json — test period, universe, feature set specification

Output JSON format (required):
  {
    "ic_mean": 0.045,
    "icir": 0.35,
    "rank_ic_mean": 0.052,
    "per_day": [
      {"date": "2022-01-04", "ic": 0.08, "rank_ic": 0.09},
      ...
    ],
    "total_days": 480
  }

ic_mean: mean daily IC across all trading days in the test period.
icir: IC mean / IC std (Information Coefficient Information Ratio).
rank_ic_mean: mean daily Spearman rank correlation.

name: qm9
summary:
Build a regression model to predict the HOMO-LUMO gap of small organic molecules.
The dataset contains ~130,831 molecules from QM9 (after excluding 3,054 uncharacterized
molecules), split 80/10/10 into train (~104,664), val (~13,083), and test (~13,084).
description:
Build a regression model to predict the HOMO-LUMO gap of small organic molecules.
The dataset contains ~130,831 molecules from QM9 (after excluding 3,054 uncharacterized
molecules), split 80/10/10 into train (~104,664), val (~13,083), and test (~13,084).

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container). Two formats are provided:

### Option A: SMILES (for 2D graph or fingerprint methods)
  - train.csv: CSV with columns "smiles" (molecular SMILES string) and "target" (HOMO-LUMO gap in eV)
  - val.csv:   same format

### Option B: 3D Coordinates (for geometric deep learning — recommended for best results)
  - train_3d.pt: PyTorch file, load with torch.load(). Contains a list of molecule dicts:
      [{"z": tensor([6,1,1,...]),         # atomic numbers (int64, shape [N_atoms])
        "pos": tensor([[x,y,z],...]),      # DFT-optimized 3D coordinates in Angstrom (float32, shape [N_atoms, 3])
        "smiles": "C...",                  # canonical SMILES string
        "target": 6.42}, ...]              # HOMO-LUMO gap in eV (float)
  - val_3d.pt: same format

3D coordinates are DFT-optimized geometries (B3LYP/6-31G(2df,p)) from the original QM9 dataset.
Molecules contain up to 9 heavy atoms (C, H, O, N, F), with ~18 atoms on average including hydrogens.
The target is the HOMO-LUMO gap in eV, ranging approximately 0.7 to 13.7 eV (mean ~6.8 eV).

State-of-the-art methods use 3D coordinates with equivariant geometric neural networks
(SchNet, DimeNet++, PaiNN, Equiformer), achieving MAE ~0.03–0.06 eV.
SMILES-only methods are limited to MAE ~0.12–0.14 eV.

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>

Input: test_data_dir contains:
  - test.csv:  column "smiles" only (no target)
  - test_3d.pt: list of dicts with keys "z", "pos", "smiles" (no target)
  Use whichever format your model requires.
Output: JSON file with format {"predictions": [4.21, 6.78, 3.14, ...]}
        One predicted HOMO-LUMO gap value (float, in eV) per molecule, in row order.

name: screenspot_pro
summary:
Train a GUI grounding model that locates UI targets from natural-language
instructions in professional desktop software screenshots, evaluated on the
held-out ScreenSpot-Pro test set.
description:
Train a GUI grounding model that locates UI targets from natural-language
instructions in professional desktop software screenshots, evaluated on the
held-out ScreenSpot-Pro test set.

## Compute & data layout

Training data lives at `/data` inside the sandbox container (= `FARBENCH_DATA_DIR`;
the bytes are packaged in the Docker image):

  /data/
    unified_train.jsonl        # ~7100 rows
    unified_val.jsonl          # ~400  rows  (image-disjoint split)
    images/<image_id>.png      # ~1500 unique source screenshots
    dataset_info.json
    base_model/
      Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct/  # pre-snapshotted, load offline

Test data lives at `<test_data_dir>` (= `/data` in the eval container):

  test_prompts.jsonl    # visible to the agent (no labels)
  test_labels.jsonl     # hidden, evaluator-only
  images/<image_id>.png
  dataset_info.json

Everything is materialized into the task image under `/rab_data`; FARBench maps
`/data` to the train split in the sandbox and to the test split during eval. The container has
`network_access: false`, so do NOT try to download anything at runtime —
Qwen2.5-VL-3B is already on disk.

## Training data: showlab/ShowUI-desktop

Source repo: `showlab/ShowUI-desktop` (a.k.a. ShowUI-desktop-8K), originally
sourced from OmniAct and re-captioned by GPT-4o into multiple instruction
variants per UI element (`type` ∈ {original, appearance, spatial, intent}).
We re-format every row into the same JSONL schema as the test set, convert
bbox/point from ShowUI's normalized [0,1] coords to ABSOLUTE pixels, and
hash-split by `image_url` so augmented variants of the same screenshot stay
in the same split (no train→val leakage).

## Test data: likaixin/ScreenSpot-Pro

Held-out professional-software grounding benchmark (CAD, 3D modeling,
scientific computing, IDEs, ...). Same JSONL schema as training data; the
evaluator joins on `id`.

## JSONL row schema (train, val, test_prompts, test_labels)

  {
    "id":           "showui-XXXXXXXXXXXX-r000123" | "sspro-000001",
    "image_path":   "images/<image_id>.png",     # relative to its dir
    "instruction":  "Click the Run button",      # absent in test_labels
    "target_bbox":  [x1, y1, x2, y2],            # ABSOLUTE pixels, XYXY (optional)
    "target_point": [x, y],                       # ABSOLUTE pixels, XY    (optional)
    "image_width":  1920,
    "image_height": 1080,
    "source":       "ShowUI-desktop" | "ScreenSpot-Pro",
    "type":         "original" | "appearance" | "spatial" | "intent"   # train only
  }

Each row carries either `target_bbox`, `target_point`, or both. `predict.py`
may answer with either form per sample.

## Predict script contract

  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <ckpt_dir> --output <output.json>

Inputs the script can read:
  - <test_data_dir>/test_prompts.jsonl
  - <test_data_dir>/images/*.png
  - <test_data_dir>/dataset_info.json
(test_labels.jsonl is NOT mounted into the eval container by FARBench.)

Required output JSON:
  {
    "predictions": [
      {"id": "sspro-000001", "bbox":  [x1, y1, x2, y2]},
      {"id": "sspro-000002", "point": [x, y]},
      ...
    ]
  }

Coordinates MUST be absolute pixels (XYXY for bbox, XY for point), clipped
to image bounds. Either `bbox` or `point` per sample is accepted; if both
are provided, the evaluator prefers the modality matching the ground truth.

## Evaluation metric

`grounding_score` = (#correct) / (#test samples), where a prediction counts
as correct iff:
  - bbox vs. target_bbox: IoU ≥ 0.5, OR
  - point vs. target_point: L2 distance ≤ 14 px.
Missing or invalid predictions count as wrong (not skipped).

Auxiliary metrics returned alongside: `mean_iou` (over bbox samples),
`mean_point_distance_px` (over point samples), `num_correct`, `total`,
`num_missing_or_invalid`.

name: split_cifar100
summary:
Build a continual learning system for class-incremental image classification on CIFAR-100.
The 100 classes are split into 10 sequential tasks (10 classes each). You must train
on tasks sequentially — train on task 0, then task 1, ..., then task 9 — and finally
classify ALL test images across all 100 classes. This is the Class-Incremental Learning
(Class-IL) scenario: task identity is NOT provided at test time.
description:
Build a continual learning system for class-incremental image classification on CIFAR-100.
The 100 classes are split into 10 sequential tasks (10 classes each). You must train
on tasks sequentially — train on task 0, then task 1, ..., then task 9 — and finally
classify ALL test images across all 100 classes. This is the Class-Incremental Learning
(Class-IL) scenario: task identity is NOT provided at test time.

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - meta.json: task metadata with fields:
      "num_tasks": 10
      "classes_per_task": [[c0,...,c9], [c10,...,c19], ...] (10 lists of 10 class indices)
      "total_classes": 100
    The class ordering is a fixed random permutation of 0-99.
  - task_0/train.pt through task_9/train.pt: per-task training data.
    Each file is a dict with keys:
      "images": uint8 tensor [N, 3, 32, 32] (RGB, pixel values 0-255)
      "labels": int64 tensor [N] (original CIFAR-100 class indices 0-99)
    Each task has ~4,500 images (10 classes x ~450 images/class).
  - val.pt: global validation set across ALL 100 classes — same dict format, ~5,000 images.
    Use this to monitor overall accuracy and detect catastrophic forgetting during sequential training.

Total: ~45,000 train images across 10 tasks, ~5,000 val images, 10,000 test images.
All images are 32x32 RGB. Normalize yourself (CIFAR-100 mean=[0.5071,0.4867,0.4408], std=[0.2675,0.2565,0.2761]).
Labels are original CIFAR-100 class indices (0-99), NOT remapped per-task.

You may use any model architecture and continual learning strategy.

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>

Input: --data_path is a DIRECTORY. Load test data with:
         test_data = torch.load(os.path.join(args.data_path, 'test.pt'))
       Use ONLY the "images" key (labels are ground truth, never use them).
       The test set contains 10,000 images from ALL 100 classes (100 per class).
Output: JSON file with format {"predictions": [42, 7, 91, 0, ...]}
        One predicted class index (int 0-99) per test sample, in order.

name: terra_incognita
summary: Build a wildlife image classifier that generalizes from 3 camera trap locations (L38, L43, L46) to a held-out location (L100). This is a domain generalization task: backgrounds, lighting, and species distributions differ drastically across locations. Evaluated by balanced accuracy (per-class recall macro-average) — the test set is heavily class-imbalanced, so correctly classifying rare species matters as much as common ones.
description:
Build a wildlife image classifier that generalizes from 3 camera trap locations (L38, L43, L46) to a held-out location (L100). This is a domain generalization task: backgrounds, lighting, and species distributions differ drastically across locations. Evaluated by balanced accuracy (per-class recall macro-average) — the test set is heavily class-imbalanced, so correctly classifying rare species matters as much as common ones.

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - train.txt: 15,914 lines of "relative_path label" (e.g., "location_38/bird/00001.jpg 0")
  - val.txt:   3,991 lines, same format (stratified 80/20 split from source domains)
  - images/:   JPEG image files organized by location/class subdirectories
  - class_names.txt: 10 class names, one per line (line index = class index)
  - domain_labels.txt: per-sample domain ID matching train.txt line order (0=L38, 1=L43, 2=L46)

10 classes (alphabetical, index 0-9): bird, bobcat, cat, coyote, dog, empty, opossum, rabbit, raccoon, squirrel. Class distribution is highly imbalanced and differs across domains. Images are variable-size JPEG (~300-600px), recommend resizing to 224x224. Domain labels enable domain-aware methods (CORAL, DANN, etc.) but are optional.

Test set: 4,879 images from L100. Class distribution is very skewed (raccoon ~52%, opossum ~20%, 6 classes each <5%).

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>

Input: test_data_dir contains test.txt with lines of "relative_path" only (no labels) and images/ directory.
Output: JSON file with format {"predictions": [3, 0, 9, 5, ...]}
        One predicted class index (int 0-9) per test sample, in order.

name: vlabench_manipulation
summary:
Train a vision-language-action manipulation policy on the FARBench VLABench
training set; evaluated by `success_rate` over 60 official-track rollouts
(3 benchmark tasks × 20 episodes) in the VLABench MuJoCo simulator.
description:
Train a vision-language-action manipulation policy on the FARBench VLABench
training set; evaluated by `success_rate` over 60 official-track rollouts
(3 benchmark tasks × 20 episodes) in the VLABench MuJoCo simulator.

## Data Format

Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (mounted as /data in the agent container):

    /data/vlabench_train/
        dataset_info.json        # FARBench manifest; read first
        schema.json              # unified episode schema
        meta/tasks.json          # per-task metadata
        meta/dataset_info.json   # source-dataset metadata
        train/task_NNN/episode_NNNNNN.hdf5 # 40 task dirs (task_000–task_039), 1693 HDF5 episodes total
    /data/vlabench_root/
        assets/                  # MuJoCo meshes / scenes used by VLABench

`dataset_info.json` must report `training_data_prepared: true`; if not, the
preparation step failed and the run should stop instead of falling back to
a heuristic.

Each episode HDF5 file (FARBench unified VLA schema, converted from
`lerobot/libero`) layout:

    observation/rgb      uint8,   shape (T, 2, H, W, 3)
    observation/state    float32, shape (T, 8)
    action               float32, shape (T, 7)
    task_index           int64 scalar
    instruction          h5py object dtype, scalar — read as
                         f["instruction"][()].decode("utf-8")
    source_dataset       h5py object dtype, scalar (identifies LIBERO
                         source) — same .decode("utf-8") pattern

    attrs:
        fps                                   float (10.0 in current data)
        schema_version                        "farbench-vla-unified-v1"
        state_dim                             8
        action_dim                            7
        num_frames                            int (varies per episode)
        view_names_json                       JSON-string '["image", "image2"]'
                                              (parse with json.loads if needed)

Sample episode shapes (from /data/vlabench_train/train/task_000/episode_000000.hdf5):
rgb=(214, 2, 256, 256, 3), state=(214, 8), action=(214, 7),
instruction="put the white mug on the left plate and put the yellow and
white mug on the right plate". Read H/W from the file (do not hardcode).

Note: this training schema (2 views, 8-D state, 7-D LIBERO EE-style action)
is NOT the same as the live VLABench simulator runtime, which exposes a
9-D Franka action spec. See `agent_hints` for the policy/action interface
contract and the Pi0 pretrained-checkpoint feature contract.

Test config is at $FARBENCH_TEST_DATA_DIR (mounted as /data during evaluation):

    /data/eval_config.json   # benchmark_tasks, episodes_per_task,
                             # max_steps_per_episode, seed,
                             # eval_track="track_1_in_distribution",
                             # eval_track_path, evaluation_protocol

## Predict Script Contract

Create predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <ckpt_dir> --output <output.json>

Input: --data_path is the directory containing eval_config.json. Run the
3 benchmark tasks listed there using the official VLABench
`track_1_in_distribution` protocol (60 episodes total). The official base
evaluator is imported as `from VLABench.evaluation.evaluator.base import
Evaluator` (the image patches `VLABench.evaluation` and
`VLABench.evaluation.evaluator` to re-export this same class). Naked
random `load_env(task)` sampling is only acceptable for local smoke tests,
not for the final eval.

Output: JSON file with one record per rollout:
  {
    "episodes": [
      {"task": "add_condiment", "episode_index": 0, "success": true},
      ...
    ],
    "total_episodes": 60
  }
Aggregated success_rate-only outputs are rejected; the evaluator
recomputes success_rate and per-task success from the per-episode records.

name: voicebank_demand
summary: Build a single-channel speech enhancement model on the VoiceBank+DEMAND dataset — given a noisy speech waveform, produce a clean (denoised) waveform. Training data contains paired clean/noisy utterances at various SNR levels. The test set uses unseen speakers and unseen noise types at different SNR levels.
description:
Build a single-channel speech enhancement model on the VoiceBank+DEMAND dataset — given a noisy speech waveform, produce a clean (denoised) waveform. Training data contains paired clean/noisy utterances at various SNR levels. The test set uses unseen speakers and unseen noise types at different SNR levels.

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - clean/:  clean speech WAV files (16 kHz, mono), 10,415 files from 28 speakers (11,572 total training utterances minus 1,157 held-out for validation)
  - noisy/:  corresponding noisy speech WAV files, same filenames as clean/
  - val_clean/:  1,157 clean validation WAV files (10% of 11,572 original training utterances, split with fixed seed 42)
  - val_noisy/:  1,157 noisy validation WAV files, same filenames as val_clean/

Each noisy file is the corresponding clean file mixed with noise from the DEMAND database at SNR levels 0/5/10/15 dB. Filenames match between clean/ and noisy/ (and val_clean/ and val_noisy/) directories. Use the validation set for hyperparameter tuning, early stopping, or model selection.

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>

Input: test_data_dir contains noisy/ directory with 824 noisy test WAV files (16 kHz mono) and test_files.txt (one filename per line, defines evaluation order).
Output: Save enhanced WAV files (16 kHz, mono) to a directory, then write a JSON file: {"predictions_dir": "<absolute_path_to_enhanced_wavs>/"}
        Enhanced files must have the same filenames as the noisy inputs. Do NOT resample — keep 16 kHz.

name: weatherbench_z500t850
summary: Build a global weather forecasting model on WeatherBench (5.625deg, 32x64 lat-lon grid) to predict Z500 (geopotential at 500 hPa) and T850 (temperature at 850 hPa) fields 72 hours (3 days) ahead from 3 consecutive 6-hourly input snapshots.
description:
Build a global weather forecasting model on WeatherBench (5.625deg, 32x64 lat-lon grid) to predict Z500 (geopotential at 500 hPa) and T850 (temperature at 850 hPa) fields 72 hours (3 days) ahead from 3 consecutive 6-hourly input snapshots.

## Data Format
Training data is at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container):
  - train_data.npy: float32 [54056, 2, 32, 64] — 6-hourly snapshots from 1979-2015 (37 years). Channel 0 = Z500 (geopotential, m^2/s^2, range ~49000-58000). Channel 1 = T850 (temperature, K, range ~230-310).
  - val_data.npy: float32 [1464, 2, 32, 64] — 6-hourly snapshots from 2016 (1 leap year).
  - lat.npy: float64 [32] — latitude values from ~87.2 deg N to ~87.2 deg S (descending).
  - lon.npy: float64 [64] — longitude values from 0 deg to ~354.4 deg E (ascending, 5.625 deg spacing).
  - norm_stats.json: {"z500_mean": float, "z500_std": float, "t850_mean": float, "t850_std": float} — global mean/std from training set for each variable.

The 32x64 grid covers the entire globe at 5.625 deg equirectangular resolution. Longitude is periodic (wraps around at 0/360 deg). Grid cells near the equator cover more area than those near the poles — the evaluation metric accounts for this via latitude weighting. To create training pairs: use sliding windows of 3 consecutive timesteps as input [3, 2, 32, 64] and the snapshot 12 steps (72h) later as target [2, 32, 64].

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>

Input: test_data_dir contains test_inputs.npy — float32 [2906, 3, 2, 32, 64]. 2906 input windows, each with 3 consecutive 6-hourly snapshots (t-12h, t-6h, t), 2 channels (Z500, T850), 32x64 grid. Values are raw (not normalized). Also available: norm_stats.json (same as training dir) and lat.npy for reference.
Output: Two things:
  1. Save predictions as a numpy file at <output_stem>_predictions.npy (where <output_stem> is the --output path without .json extension), shape [2906, 2, 32, 64], float32. Channel 0 = predicted Z500 (m^2/s^2), Channel 1 = predicted T850 (K). Values must be in original (raw) units.
  2. Write the JSON file at --output path: {"predictions_file": "<absolute_path_to_npy>"}

## Data Format

Training data at $FARBENCH_DATA_DIR (/data in container, ~45-50 GB on disk):
  - train.txt            : N_train lines of "rgb_img_{idx}.png {label}" (space-separated, label ∈ [0,61], whitespace-free)
  - val.txt              : N_val   lines of "rgb_img_{idx}.png {label}" (OOD val, 2013-2015)
  - region_labels_train.txt : N_train lines, one integer per line (aligned by line index to train.txt)
  - region_labels_val.txt   : N_val   lines, same format as above
  - class_names.txt      : 62 lines, one class name per line (line index == class index 0-61)
  - images/              : flat directory of .png files (real files, ~96K total = train + val combined); fully self-contained, no external dependencies

Test data at $FARBENCH_TEST_DATA_DIR (/data in the eval container), given to predict.py:
  - test.txt             : N_test lines of "rgb_img_{idx}.png" ONLY — no label column
  - images/              : flat directory with the test PNGs
  (test_labels.txt and test_regions.txt also live in this directory; they are used by the evaluator. predict.py MUST only read test.txt and images/ — reading the label files is considered cheating.)

Image spec (identical for all splits):
  - Format:      PNG, RGB, 3 channels, 8-bit unsigned (pixel range 0-255)
  - Size:        224 × 224 (already resized by WILDS v1.1; standard ImageNet input size)
  - Filename:    rgb_img_{idx}.png where {idx} is the original row index in WILDS rgb_metadata.csv (gaps are normal — do NOT assume contiguous indices)
  - Content:     RGB satellite imagery captured between 2002-2017; resolution of the source imagery varies but all samples are resampled to 224×224
  - Normalization: pixel values are raw uint8 (0-255), NOT pre-normalized. Apply your model's own mean/std normalization after loading.

Region encoding (regions derived from country_code via country_code_mapping.csv):
  0 = Africa
  1 = Americas
  2 = Asia
  3 = Europe
  4 = Oceania
 -1 = Other / Unknown country (excluded from worst-region metric but still appears in train/val — very rare, ~0.05% of train and ~0.02% of test based on actual prepared data)
  Region label files are ONE integer per line, aligned to the corresponding .txt file by line index. Using region metadata during training is optional — the predict.py contract does not require it.

Label encoding:
  - Labels are integers in [0, 61] covering 62 FMoW land-use categories (airport, aquaculture, barn, crop_field, educational_institution, hospital, shopping_mall, stadium, wind_farm, zoo, ...).
  - See class_names.txt for the full canonical list (line N = class N).
  - Class distribution is heavily imbalanced — popular classes (crop_field, single-unit_residential, educational_institution) have 10-20K samples; rare classes (helipad, debris_or_rubble, nuclear_powerplant) have a few hundred.
  - Year and region distributions are also imbalanced — training data is skewed toward Americas & earlier years; OOD test spans years 2016-2017 across all 5 regions.

Why this task is hard (context only, no method implied):
  - Temporal distribution shift: cities grow, construction sites become buildings, landscapes change 2002→2017
  - Geographic shift: regions differ systematically in building styles, vegetation, and infrastructure; the training set is NOT uniformly distributed across regions
  - Worst-region metric penalizes models that fit majority regions well but fail on minority regions

## Predict Script Contract
You must create a predict.py that accepts these arguments:
  python predict.py --data_path <test_data_dir> --checkpoint <model_checkpoint> --output <output.json>

Input: <test_data_dir> contains test.txt (one "rgb_img_{idx}.png" per line, NO labels) and images/ (the .png files referenced by test.txt).
Output: JSON file at <output.json> with format {"predictions": [14, 0, 55, 3, ...]}
        One predicted class index (int in [0,61]) per test sample, in the SAME order as test.txt lines. Length MUST equal len(test.txt).
